alternatives.name: localStorage with JSON serialization (in-browser, zero-dependency)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are serialized as JSON and stored in the browser's localStorage under namespaced keys (e.g., `cashpulse:expenses`, `cashpulse:categories`). A thin repository layer (8 files, one per entity) wraps read/write/delete operations. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly from raw Expense records at query time and optionally memoized in React state or a lightweight cache (e.g., useMemo). QuickAddSuggestions are derived from recent Expense frequency counts. No build-time or runtime dependencies are added.
tradeoffs: ZERO new dependencies — no npm packages, no build config changes
Synchronous read/write API means no async loading states to manage in UI, keeping micro-interactions well under 150ms
Storage cap of ~5MB per origin; with 8 entities and typical expense volumes (~10 expenses/day × 365 days = ~3,650 records/year), JSON overhead stays well under 1MB for several years of data
No query language — filtering/sorting/aggregation for Insights charts must be hand-written JS; adds ~200–400 lines of utility code across the repository layer
Data is device-local and non-portable (aligns with scope-exclusion: no cloud sync, no backup)
No schema migrations — adding fields to entities requires a one-time migration function run on app boot (low risk for greenfield)
Blast radius: 8 new repository files + 1 shared storage utility = 9 files; 0 existing files modified (greenfield)

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (in-browser, structured, queryable)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as object stores in IndexedDB, accessed through Dexie.js (a well-maintained, typed IndexedDB wrapper, ~25KB gzipped). Each entity maps to a Dexie table with declared indexes (e.g., Expense indexed on `date`, `categoryId`, `amount`). Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's `.where().between()` and `.filter()` APIs. Dexie's `liveQuery` hook enables reactive UI updates. Schema versioning is built-in, making future entity changes safe. QuickAddSuggestions are derived from a Dexie query on recent Expense records grouped by description.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: `dexie` (~25KB gzipped); optionally `dexie-react-hooks` for liveQuery (~2KB)
Asynchronous API requires async/await patterns and loading state management in components — adds ~15–20 component-level state changes

IndexedDB supports structured queries with indexes, making Insights aggregations (date ranges, category grouping) significantly faster than localStorage JSON scanning — chart renders comfortably within 600ms even with 10,000+ records
Built-in schema versioning (Dexie `version().stores()`) handles future entity field additions cleanly with zero data loss
Storage quota is much larger than localStorage (~60% of available disk space in Chrome), supporting years of expense data
Dexie's `liveQuery` enables real-time reactive updates across screens (e.g., Dashboard auto-refreshes after Add Expense) without manual cache invalidation
Blast radius: 1 `db.ts` schema file + 8 repository files + 1 package.json change = 10 files; 0 existing files modified (greenfield)

alternatives.name: Zustand in-memory store with sessionStorage persistence (ephemeral, state-first)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities live in a Zustand global store (sliced per entity). The `persist` middleware serializes the full store to sessionStorage on every mutation, providing tab-session durability without cross-session persistence. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed as Zustand selectors using `useShallow` to prevent unnecessary re-renders. This approach treats the store as the single source of truth and co-locates data access with UI state management — no separate repository layer needed.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: `zustand` (~3KB gzipped) — likely already planned for UI state management, making this effectively zero net-new dependencies

Data is lost on browser close/refresh (sessionStorage scope) — acceptable only if the PRD treats CashPulse as a demo/prototype; NOT suitable for production use where users expect data persistence across sessions
Synchronous reads from Zustand store guarantee all micro-interactions stay under 150ms with no async overhead

Zustand selectors for derived entities (e.g., monthly totals) recompute on every relevant mutation — for large datasets (1,000+ expenses) this may cause perceptible lag in Insights screen without explicit memoization
sessionStorage cap is ~5MB, same constraints as localStorage
No schema migration path — store shape changes require clearing sessionStorage on version bump
Blast radius: 8 Zustand slice files + 1 store root file = 9 files; 0 existing files modified (greenfield)

alternatives.name: localStorage (primary) + Dexie.js IndexedDB (overflow/archive) hybrid
alternatives.description: Recent data (current month's expenses, active budgets, user settings, quick-add suggestions) is kept in localStorage for synchronous, zero-latency access powering all daily interactions. Historical data (expenses older than 30 days, archived monthly summaries) is offloaded to IndexedDB via Dexie.js. The Insights screen queries IndexedDB for historical aggregations. A background migration job runs on app boot to move aged records from localStorage to IndexedDB. This hybrid maximizes UI responsiveness for the 95% case (daily use) while supporting rich historical analytics.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: `dexie` (~25KB gzipped)
Introduces a two-tier data access pattern — developers must know which tier to query for which data; adds cognitive overhead and ~500 lines of orchestration code
Migration job on boot adds ~50–100ms to initial load time (acceptable, but must be measured)
Significantly more complex than either single-store alternative — 2 storage layers, 1 migration service, 8 repository files with dual-read logic = ~18 files total
Overkill for a single-user desktop app with typical expense volumes; the complexity is only justified if the PRD explicitly targets multi-year historical analytics
Blast radius: 8 repository files + 1 Dexie schema + 1 migration service + 1 package.json = 11 files; 0 existing files modified (greenfield)

structured.recommendation: alt-2 — IndexedDB via Dexie.js. It is the single best fit for CashPulse's full feature set: structured queries with indexes make Insights aggregations fast and correct, built-in schema versioning eliminates migration risk as the 8 entities evolve, `liveQuery` provides reactive cross-screen updates (Dashboard refreshes instantly after Add Expense), and the storage quota is effectively unlimited for a personal expense tracker. The blast radius is identical to the localStorage-only option (10 files, all new), and the single added dependency (Dexie, 25KB) is well-maintained, TypeScript-native, and widely adopted. The async API is the only meaningful tradeoff, but React's Suspense or simple `isLoading` guards handle this cleanly without impacting the 150ms micro-interaction budget.
structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency/synchronous (localStorage, Zustand) to structured/queryable (Dexie.js) to hybrid. All are implementable entirely in-browser with no backend, satisfying the hard scope-exclusion constraints (no backend API, no cloud sync, no auth). SQLite (via sql.js or wa-sqlite) was excluded because it adds a ~1MB WASM binary, requires SharedArrayBuffer (cross-origin isolation headers), and provides no meaningful advantage over Dexie.js for a single-user app. Supabase/PostgreSQL was excluded because the PRD explicitly scopes out backend API development and cloud storage. Firebase/Firestore was excluded for the same reason (cloud sync out of scope). The Zustand-only option (alt-3) is included as the minimal-change alternative since Zustand is likely already in the stack, but its sessionStorage-only persistence makes it unsuitable as the primary data store for a production expense tracker.

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under the 'extend' key. All components — cards, chips, popovers, form inputs — are styled via utility classes directly in JSX. The 4px base spacing unit maps to Tailwind's default scale (space-1=4px). Custom values for card-radius (16px → rounded-2xl override), button-radius (10px → custom), badge-radius (20px → custom), and the two shadow tokens are added to the config. Responsive breakpoints (768px, 1024px, 1440px) are defined as custom screens. prefers-reduced-motion is handled via the 'motion-reduce:' variant. Focus rings use ring-2 ring-blue-600 ring-offset-2 utilities. Inter font is set globally via fontFamily config.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero runtime CSS-in-JS overhead — all styles are static at build time, directly supporting the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms transition performance budgets
PROS: PurgeCSS/content scanning ships only used classes — final CSS bundle typically <10KB gzipped for a 7-screen app of this scope
PROS: Responsive variants (md:, lg:, xl:) and motion-reduce: variant natively handle all three breakpoints and the prefers-reduced-motion constraint with no extra code
PROS: Greenfield project — no migration cost; tailwind.config.js is the single source of truth for all design tokens
CONS: Adds 1 new dependency (tailwindcss) plus 2 peer deps (postcss, autoprefixer) — 3 total new packages
CONS: Long className strings on complex components (e.g., the Add Expense form card) reduce JSX readability; mitigated by extracting repeated patterns into component abstractions
CONS: Design token enforcement is convention-based — developers can still use arbitrary values (e.g., w-[13px]) bypassing the token system; requires lint rules (eslint-plugin-tailwindcss) to enforce
CONS: Tailwind's default scale does not include 10px (button-radius) or 20px (badge-radius) out of the box — requires explicit config entries and team awareness of custom class names

alternatives.name: CSS Modules + CSS Custom Properties (Token-Native)
alternatives.description: Each component gets a co-located .module.css file. All design tokens are declared as CSS custom properties in a global :root block (globals.css): --radius-card: 16px, --radius-button: 10px, --radius-badge: 20px, --shadow-card: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06), --shadow-elevated: 0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.10), --color-primary: #2563eb (blue-600), --spacing-base: 4px, --font-family: 'Inter', sans-serif. Components import styles from './Component.module.css' and reference var(--token-name). Responsive breakpoints are written as standard @media queries. prefers-reduced-motion is a @media block in globals.css that sets transition-duration: 0ms on all animated elements. Focus rings are defined once in a shared .focusRing class and composed via composes.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero new runtime dependencies — CSS Modules are natively supported by Vite, Next.js, and CRA; no postcss plugins required beyond what ships by default
PROS: CSS custom properties are the most direct encoding of the design token spec — token names map 1:1 to the PRD's token table, making design-dev handoff unambiguous
PROS: Locally scoped class names eliminate all specificity conflicts across 7 screens and 25 features without any naming convention overhead
PROS: Minimal blast radius — styling is entirely co-located; changing a component's styles touches only its .module.css file
CONS: Adds 0 new dependencies but requires discipline: ~25-40 .module.css files for a 7-screen app, increasing file count and cognitive overhead vs. co-located utility classes
CONS: No built-in purging — unused CSS rules in .module.css files are not automatically removed (though CSS Modules scoping limits leakage); bundle size slightly larger than Tailwind's purged output
CONS: Sharing styles across components (e.g., the focus ring pattern applied to all 36×36px interactive targets) requires either composes: or a shared utility CSS file — two patterns to maintain
CONS: Responsive design requires duplicating breakpoint values across multiple .module.css files; a PostCSS custom-media plugin can centralize them but adds 1 dependency

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS Runtime)
alternatives.description: All UI components are styled using tagged template literals via styled-components. A ThemeProvider wraps the app root and injects the full design token object (spacing, radii, shadows, colors, typography) as a typed theme prop accessible in every styled component. Focus rings, transition timings, and prefers-reduced-motion overrides are written as shared CSS fragments (css helper) and composed into components. The theme object serves as the authoritative token registry. TypeScript theme typing via DefaultTheme ensures token misuse is caught at compile time.
tradeoffs: PROS: ThemeProvider gives the strongest token enforcement story — accessing theme.radius.card in a styled component is a compile-time-checked reference, not a string convention
PROS: Dynamic styling based on props (e.g., a Budget chip that changes color based on percentage spent) is ergonomic without extra className logic or CSS custom property updates
PROS: Co-located styles and component logic in a single .tsx file reduces context switching for complex interactive components like the Add Expense form
CONS: Adds 2 new dependencies (styled-components, @types/styled-components) and requires Babel plugin or SWC transform for SSR/display names — 2-3 packages total

CONS: Runtime CSS injection introduces ~10-30ms style recalculation overhead per component mount; risks breaching the 150ms micro-interaction budget on lower-end hardware for complex re-renders (e.g., chart entry animations with many styled nodes)

CONS: styled-components v6 drops IE11 support (acceptable per constraints) but the runtime still generates and injects <style> tags dynamically, which can cause FOUC in SSR contexts if not configured with ServerStyleSheet
CONS: Bundle size impact: styled-components adds ~12KB gzipped to the JS bundle — larger than Tailwind's purged CSS or CSS Modules output
CONS: Debugging in DevTools shows generated class names (sc-abc123) rather than semantic names unless the Babel plugin is configured — adds setup complexity

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS + CVA (Class Variance Authority) — Hybrid Token-Safe
alternatives.description: Extends alt-tailwind with Class Variance Authority (CVA) to create a typed variant API for all design system primitives. Each reusable component (Button, Card, Badge, Input, Chip) is defined with cva() specifying base classes and named variants (e.g., size: 'sm' | 'md', intent: 'primary' | 'ghost'). This eliminates raw className string concatenation in JSX and enforces the token system at the TypeScript level. tailwind.config.js still holds all token overrides. The tailwind-merge utility handles class conflict resolution when variants are composed. This is the recommended production pattern for Tailwind in design-system-grade projects.
tradeoffs: PROS: Adds only 2 small dependencies (cva ~2KB, tailwind-merge ~3KB) on top of Tailwind — total new packages: 5 (tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer, cva, tailwind-merge)
PROS: CVA's TypeScript variant API makes it impossible to pass an invalid variant value (e.g., size='xs' when only 'sm'|'md' are defined) — strongest DX token enforcement of all alternatives
PROS: Retains all Tailwind performance benefits (zero runtime, purged CSS) while solving the readability and token-drift problems of raw Tailwind
PROS: Scales cleanly across 25 features and 7 screens — each primitive component is self-documenting via its cva() definition, which doubles as a component API contract

CONS: Highest initial setup cost of the Tailwind options — requires authoring cva() definitions for every primitive before feature development begins (estimated 1-2 days for Button, Card, Badge, Input, Chip, Chip group, Focus ring)
CONS: Two mental models to hold: Tailwind utility classes for layout/spacing and CVA variant names for component states — steeper onboarding for developers unfamiliar with CVA
CONS: tailwind-merge adds a runtime function call per component render for class deduplication — negligible (~0.1ms) but non-zero, unlike pure CSS Modules
CONS: Storybook or similar component explorer is strongly recommended to document variants — without it, the variant API is only discoverable via TypeScript autocomplete

structured.rationale: These four alternatives cover the full spectrum of viable CSS methodologies for a greenfield desktop-first React app in 2024: (1) pure utility-first, (2) pure CSS-native with zero dependencies, (3) CSS-in-JS runtime, and (4) utility-first with a typed variant layer. Excluded alternatives: Sass/SCSS — adds a preprocessor dependency with no meaningful advantage over CSS custom properties for token management in a modern bundler environment. Emotion — functionally equivalent to styled-components but with a smaller ecosystem; no differentiated tradeoff worth a separate slot. Vanilla Extract — zero-runtime CSS-in-JS is compelling but requires a Vite/Next plugin and has a steeper learning curve with limited community resources compared to the four listed options. Stitches — effectively unmaintained as of 2023. The four selected alternatives span the key decision axes relevant to CashPulse's constraints: runtime performance (critical for 150ms micro-interaction budget), token enforcement fidelity (critical for the strict design system spec), dependency footprint, and developer ergonomics at the 7-screen / 25-feature scale.
structured.recommendation: alt-tailwind-cva — Tailwind CSS + CVA is the strongest fit for CashPulse. The strict design token spec (7 distinct radius values, 2 shadow tokens, precise focus ring rules, 3 breakpoints) demands a token enforcement mechanism beyond naming conventions, which CVA's TypeScript variant API provides. The zero-runtime nature of Tailwind directly protects the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms transition budgets. The prefers-reduced-motion and focus ring constraints are handled by Tailwind's built-in variants with no extra code. The 5-package dependency cost is the highest of the utility-first options but remains lower than styled-components' runtime overhead. For a greenfield project with a well-defined design system, the 1-2 day upfront cost of authoring CVA primitives pays dividends across all 25 features. If team familiarity with Tailwind is low or the team prefers co-located CSS files, alt-css-modules is the recommended fallback — it has zero new dependencies and the most direct token-to-CSS-variable mapping.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS)
alternatives.description: shadcn/ui is a collection of copy-paste components built on top of Radix UI headless primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS utility classes. Components are owned by the project (no runtime library dependency) — source files live directly in the repo under /components/ui. Radix primitives provide battle-tested accessibility (focus management, keyboard navigation, ARIA roles) out of the box. Tailwind enables precise token mapping for the CashPulse design system (Inter, 4px spacing unit, custom radii, shadows). For charts, pair with Recharts (already Tailwind-friendly) or shadcn/ui's own chart wrappers built on Recharts.
tradeoffs: PROS: Zero runtime bundle overhead — components are inlined, tree-shaken naturally by bundler
PROS: Radix primitives satisfy all WCAG 2.1 AA requirements (focus traps, keyboard nav, ARIA) with no extra work
PROS: Full design-token control — Tailwind config maps directly to CashPulse tokens (blue-600, Inter, radii, shadows)
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion handled via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant on every animation class
PROS: No version-lock risk — components are copied into the repo and owned by the team
CONS: Adds Tailwind CSS as a build-time dependency (PostCSS pipeline required)
CONS: Adds Radix UI as a runtime peer dependency (~12 scoped packages, ~45 KB gzipped total)
CONS: No pre-built chart components — requires a separate charting library (Recharts adds ~130 KB gzipped)
CONS: Initial scaffolding requires running the shadcn CLI to add each component (~20 component files generated across /components/ui)
CONS: Tailwind class verbosity can make JSX noisy without a consistent component abstraction layer

alternatives.name: Mantine v7 (full-featured component library)
alternatives.description: Mantine is a fully-featured React component library with 100+ components, built-in form management (@mantine/form), date pickers (@mantine/dates), charts (@mantine/charts, built on Recharts), and a CSS Modules styling system. It ships with a robust theming API that accepts design tokens directly (font family, spacing scale, radius scale, shadow scale, color palette). Accessibility is first-class. The amount input, date picker, and category chip/badge components needed for CashPulse's Add Expense form are all available out of the box.
tradeoffs: PROS: Highest feature coverage for CashPulse — NumberInput (with decimal formatting), DatePickerInput (with future-date disabling), Chip groups, Popover, Badge, and Charts are all native Mantine components
PROS: @mantine/form provides built-in validation (max value, decimal places) reducing custom validation code
PROS: @mantine/charts wraps Recharts with Mantine theming — chart renders are pre-integrated
PROS: Single theming object maps all CashPulse tokens (Inter, 4px base, radii, shadows, blue-600 primary)
PROS: prefers-reduced-motion respected via CSS custom properties and Mantine's built-in motion config
CONS: Largest bundle footprint of all alternatives — full Mantine core is ~180 KB gzipped (mitigated by tree-shaking per component)
CONS: Adds 4 new runtime dependencies: @mantine/core, @mantine/hooks, @mantine/form, @mantine/charts
CONS: CSS Modules approach means styling overrides require understanding Mantine's classNames/styles API — less flexible than utility-first for one-off tweaks
CONS: Opinionated default styles may conflict with CashPulse's strict design tokens (card shadow, button radius) requiring theme overrides in ~15 token definitions
CONS: Version upgrades (v6→v7 was breaking) introduce migration risk for a long-lived project

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI) v6 with custom theme
alternatives.description: MUI is the most widely adopted React component library, implementing Material Design with a powerful theming system. For CashPulse, the default Material Design aesthetic would be heavily overridden via MUI's theme to match the sharp, structured financial design language (Inter, blue-600, custom radii, card shadows). MUI's sx prop and styled() API allow per-component token application. MUI X Charts or Recharts handles the Insights screen. MUI's accessibility record is strong and WCAG 2.1 AA compliance is well-documented.
tradeoffs: PROS: Largest ecosystem and community — extensive documentation, Stack Overflow coverage, and long-term support guarantee
PROS: Comprehensive component set covers all CashPulse needs: TextField with inputProps for numeric validation, DatePicker (MUI X), Chip, Popover, Badge, Tooltip
PROS: MUI X Charts (bar, line, pie) are production-ready with built-in aria-label support and animation control
PROS: Emotion-based styling with sx prop allows inline token application without a separate CSS file

CONS: Material Design aesthetic is deeply embedded — overriding to CashPulse's design language requires a comprehensive theme object touching ~30+ token overrides (typography, shape, shadows, palette, components)
CONS: Heaviest bundle of all alternatives — @mui/material alone is ~90 KB gzipped; adding MUI X adds another ~60 KB
CONS: Adds 3 runtime dependencies: @mui/material, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled (Emotion is mandatory)
CONS: MUI X Charts and DatePicker require separate @mui/x-charts and @mui/x-date-pickers packages with their own versioning
CONS: Emotion's CSS-in-JS runtime has measurable overhead on first render — may challenge the 150ms micro-interaction budget on lower-end hardware
CONS: The 'Material Design look' often bleeds through overrides, creating visual inconsistency that requires ongoing theme maintenance

alternatives.name: Custom components on Radix UI primitives (minimal-change, no style framework)
alternatives.description: Use Radix UI headless primitives directly (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, etc.) combined with plain CSS Modules for styling — no Tailwind, no component library. Each CashPulse component is hand-authored to exactly match the design tokens. This is the minimal-change alternative: it introduces only Radix UI as a runtime dependency and keeps the styling layer as vanilla CSS, which is the lowest-friction approach for a greenfield project with a strict, well-defined design system. Recharts is added separately for the Insights charts.
tradeoffs: PROS: Smallest dependency surface — only Radix UI primitives (~45 KB gzipped) and Recharts (~130 KB gzipped) are added as runtime dependencies
PROS: Zero style framework overhead — CSS Modules are zero-runtime and produce the smallest possible CSS bundle
PROS: Perfect pixel control over every CashPulse token — no overrides needed, styles are written once to spec
PROS: Radix primitives provide full WCAG 2.1 AA compliance (keyboard nav, focus management, ARIA) without any configuration
PROS: No framework migration risk — plain CSS Modules are stable indefinitely

CONS: Highest initial development effort — every component (NumberInput with decimal formatting, DatePicker with future-date guard, Chip group, Badge, Card) must be built from scratch (~25–35 component files)
CONS: No built-in form validation — amount validation logic ($99,999.99 cap, 2 decimal places, silent rejection) must be hand-implemented
CONS: No pre-built date picker in Radix — requires either a custom calendar component or adding a lightweight library (react-day-picker, +~15 KB gzipped)
CONS: Ongoing maintenance burden — accessibility edge cases (e.g., focus restoration after modal close) must be manually tested rather than inherited from a maintained library
CONS: Slower iteration speed for the 25 features across 7 screens compared to library-backed alternatives

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from minimal-change (custom + Radix) to full-featured (Mantine, MUI) with a pragmatic middle ground (shadcn/ui). Excluded alternatives: (1) Chakra UI — similar positioning to Mantine but with a weaker chart story and slower v3 adoption; (2) Ant Design — enterprise-oriented aesthetic that conflicts heavily with CashPulse's sharp financial design language and carries the largest bundle of any option; (3) Headless UI (Tailwind Labs) — fewer primitives than Radix (no Select, no Tooltip, no Slider) making it insufficient for CashPulse's component needs without significant custom work. All four included alternatives are implementable in a greenfield React project with no existing tech stack constraints to work around.
structured.recommendation: alt-shadcn-ui — shadcn/ui with Radix UI + Tailwind CSS is the strongest fit for CashPulse. It delivers Radix's battle-tested accessibility (satisfying the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint with zero configuration), gives the team full ownership of component source (no version-lock risk), and maps CashPulse's strict design tokens directly to Tailwind config with surgical precision. The prefers-reduced-motion soft constraint is handled elegantly via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant. The blast radius is medium — Tailwind requires a PostCSS pipeline and ~20 component files are generated — but this is a one-time setup cost on a greenfield project. Mantine is a close second if the team wants to move faster on the Add Expense form (NumberInput + DatePickerInput are pre-built), but its larger bundle and CSS Modules override complexity make shadcn/ui the better long-term choice for a design-token-strict project like CashPulse.

alternatives.name: Dexie.js (IndexedDB wrapper) with typed repository pattern
alternatives.description: Use Dexie.js as a lightweight, promise-based IndexedDB abstraction. Define one Dexie database class with versioned schema migrations for all 8 entities. Wrap each entity in a thin typed repository (e.g., ExpenseRepository, BudgetRepository) that encapsulates query logic. Dexie's liveQuery() hook integrates directly with React state for reactive UI updates — critical for the dashboard's real-time budget status and chart data. All data lives in the browser's IndexedDB, consistent with the scope exclusion of cloud/backend storage.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: dexie@^3 (~24 KB gzipped)
IndexedDB is async-only — all reads/writes return Promises, requiring careful loading-state management across 7 screens
Schema migrations must be versioned manually; a mistake in a version bump can corrupt local data for existing users
No built-in computed/derived entity support — MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending (entities 005–007) must be computed via JS aggregation queries at read time
liveQuery() reactive subscriptions add ~1 re-render cycle overhead but stay well within the 150ms micro-interaction budget
Full-text search on expense descriptions requires manual indexing strategy
8 entity tables defined in ~1 file (db.ts); 8 repository files; affects ~16 files total

alternatives.name: Zustand store with in-memory persistence via localStorage (serialized JSON)
alternatives.description: Model all 8 entities as slices within a Zustand store. Persist the entire store to localStorage using the zustand/middleware persist plugin with JSON serialization. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed as Zustand selectors using a memoization library (e.g., reselect or Zustand's built-in selector equality). This is the 'minimal change' alternative — if the project already uses Zustand for UI state, this requires zero additional dependencies beyond the persist middleware that ships with Zustand.
tradeoffs: Adds 0 net new dependencies if Zustand is already in the stack; persist middleware is bundled with zustand
localStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5–10 MB — sufficient for personal expense data but will degrade if a user accumulates years of daily expenses (est. ~500 KB/year at 5 expenses/day)
No query language — filtering, sorting, and aggregating expenses (e.g., Insights screen charts) requires hand-written JS array operations
All 8 entity datasets are loaded into memory on app boot — fast reads (sub-1ms) but no lazy loading
localStorage stores plain JSON strings; no schema versioning or migration support — breaking data shape changes require a manual migration utility
QuickAddSuggestion (entity-008) frequency tracking and sorting is trivial to implement as a sorted array
Affects ~5–8 store slice files + ~7 screen components that consume selectors; blast radius ~15 files

alternatives.name: RxDB with reactive queries and structured schema validation
alternatives.description: Use RxDB (built on top of IndexedDB via a storage adapter) to manage all 8 entities with JSON Schema validation, reactive query streams (RxJS Observables), and built-in schema migration support. RxDB enforces entity shape at write time, making it the most robust option for data integrity — directly supporting the hard constraint on amount validation and the soft constraint on data integrity. Reactive collections integrate with React via rxdb-hooks, enabling live-updating dashboard widgets and chart data without manual cache invalidation.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: rxdb@^14 (~120 KB gzipped) and rxjs@^7 (~50 KB gzipped) — largest bundle impact of all alternatives
RxDB's learning curve is steeper than Dexie or Zustand; team must understand RxJS Observable patterns
JSON Schema definitions for all 8 entities add ~200 lines of schema boilerplate across 8 schema files
Schema migrations are first-class and versioned, eliminating the data corruption risk present in alt-01 and alt-02
Reactive queries (e.g., live expense list on Dashboard) automatically re-render components within one event loop tick — well within 150ms micro-interaction budget
Overkill for a single-user, desktop-only app with no sync requirement; the reactive/multi-tab sync features go unused given scope exclusions
Affects ~8 schema files + ~8 collection files + ~7 screen components; blast radius ~23 files

alternatives.name: sql.js (SQLite compiled to WASM) with Drizzle ORM
alternatives.description: Run a full SQLite database in the browser via sql.js (SQLite compiled to WebAssembly), with Drizzle ORM providing a type-safe, SQL-like query builder. The database is serialized to a Uint8Array and persisted to localStorage or IndexedDB on each write. This gives CashPulse a real relational query engine — enabling efficient GROUP BY aggregations for CategorySpending and DailySpending derived entities, and JOIN queries across Expense + Category + Budget. Drizzle's schema-first approach generates TypeScript types for all 8 entities automatically.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: sql.js (~1.2 MB WASM binary, loaded async) and drizzle-orm@^0.29 (~45 KB gzipped) — the WASM binary is the largest asset cost by far and must be lazy-loaded
WASM initialization adds ~200–400ms cold-start latency on first load; subsequent queries are synchronous and extremely fast
The 1.2 MB WASM binary must be served correctly with MIME type application/wasm and appropriate COOP/COEP headers if using SharedArrayBuffer
Database persistence requires manual serialize/deserialize on every write — a crash between write and persist can cause data loss; mitigated with a write-through persistence wrapper
Drizzle schema definitions for 8 entities replace hand-written repository logic with generated, type-safe query builders — reduces runtime bugs
GROUP BY queries for MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending are expressed in idiomatic SQL rather than JS array reduce — more readable and performant at scale
Affects ~8 schema/migration files + ~1 db init file + ~7 screen data-fetching hooks; blast radius ~16 files but with a heavy infrastructure setup cost

structured.rationale: All four alternatives are implementable entirely client-side, consistent with the hard scope exclusion of backend API development. Server-side ORMs (Prisma, TypeORM, Sequelize) were excluded because there is no backend. Firebase/Supabase were excluded due to the scope exclusion of cloud storage and sync. PouchDB was excluded because its primary value proposition (CouchDB sync) is out of scope, making it strictly worse than Dexie for this use case. The four selected alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency (alt-02) to full relational engine (alt-04), giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/complexity gradient to choose from. The recommendation leans toward Dexie.js (alt-01) as the best balance of capability, bundle size, and developer ergonomics for a greenfield personal finance app with 8 entities and no sync requirements.
structured.recommendation: alt-01 — Dexie.js with typed repositories. It is purpose-built for exactly this use case: a structured, multi-entity client-side database with reactive UI integration. At ~24 KB gzipped it has a negligible bundle impact, liveQuery() satisfies the real-time dashboard update requirement without additional state management complexity, and its versioned migration system is far safer than raw localStorage JSON (alt-02) without the WASM cold-start penalty of alt-04 or the RxJS complexity overhead of alt-03. The repository pattern keeps entity logic encapsulated and testable. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's compound index queries, which are fast enough to stay within the 600ms chart render budget even with 12+ months of expense history.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Store via Zustand (Client-Side State)
alternatives.description: Use Zustand (or equivalent lightweight state manager) as the single source of truth for all 8 data entities. On app load, fetch all data once and hydrate the store. Subsequent reads (dashboard renders, chart computations, Quick Add suggestions) are synchronous in-memory lookups — zero network round-trips after initial load. Writes optimistically update the store immediately, then persist to localStorage as a durable fallback. No external cache infrastructure required.
tradeoffs: Minimal blast radius: affects only the data-access layer (~3–5 store slice files + their consumer hooks)
Adds 1 dependency: zustand (~3 kB gzipped); no server-side infrastructure

All 8 entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) fit comfortably in memory for a single-user desktop app — typical dataset is <500 KB
Synchronous reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions and sub-600ms chart renders without any async overhead
Data is lost on hard refresh unless paired with localStorage persistence (adds ~20 lines of middleware)
No TTL or invalidation logic needed — store is the authoritative state; mutations are explicit
Does NOT scale to multi-user or real-time sync (both explicitly out of scope)
Requires careful selector memoization (e.g., via Zustand's useShallow) to avoid unnecessary re-renders on large expense lists

alternatives.name: HTTP Cache Headers + Service Worker (Network-Layer Cache)
alternatives.description: Serve expense data from a lightweight local or mock API (e.g., json-server, MSW, or a future real backend) with proper Cache-Control headers (e.g., Cache-Control: max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=300). Register a Service Worker using Workbox to intercept fetch requests and serve stale data instantly while revalidating in the background. This is the standard progressive-web-app caching pattern and aligns with browser-native mechanisms.
tradeoffs: Medium blast radius: requires adding a service worker registration file, a Workbox config, and cache-strategy definitions (~5–8 new files); existing fetch calls need no changes
Adds 2 dependencies: workbox-window + workbox-strategies (or the Workbox CLI); increases build complexity
stale-while-revalidate ensures dashboard and chart data appear instantly on repeat visits, satisfying the 350ms screen-transition constraint
Service Worker lifecycle (install, activate, update) introduces edge cases: stale UI after deploys requires versioned cache keys and explicit skipWaiting logic
HTTP caching is transparent to React components — no store refactoring needed, making this composable with alt-1
Requires a real or mock HTTP server to emit correct headers; pure localStorage-based apps cannot use this pattern
prefers-reduced-motion and WCAG constraints are unaffected — this is purely a network layer concern
Cache invalidation on expense write/delete requires either cache.delete() calls in the SW or short max-age values, adding ~30–50 lines of SW logic

alternatives.name: React Query / SWR with Automatic Deduplication and Background Refresh
alternatives.description: Adopt TanStack Query (React Query) or SWR as a data-fetching and caching layer. Each entity (Expense list, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, etc.) is declared as a query with a configurable staleTime and gcTime. The library handles deduplication of concurrent requests, background refetching, optimistic updates for Add/Edit/Delete flows, and automatic cache invalidation via queryClient.invalidateQueries(). This is the industry-standard pattern for React data-fetching.
tradeoffs: Medium blast radius: all data-fetching code must be refactored into useQuery/useMutation hooks — affects every screen component and any existing fetch utilities (~10–15 files)
Adds 1 dependency: @tanstack/react-query (~13 kB gzipped) + optional devtools; SWR is lighter (~4 kB) but less featured
staleTime: Infinity for reference data (Category, UserSettings) means zero re-fetches; staleTime: 30_000 for Expense list balances freshness vs. performance
Optimistic updates for Add Expense satisfy the <150ms micro-interaction constraint — UI updates before server confirmation
Built-in request deduplication prevents redundant fetches when Dashboard and Insights screens both consume MonthSummary simultaneously
Requires a real or mock API endpoint (MSW recommended for greenfield); pure localStorage usage requires a custom queryFn adapter (~20 lines per entity)
DevTools add observability but must be tree-shaken from production builds

Cache invalidation strategy must be explicitly designed: invalidating ['expenses'] after a mutation must also cascade to ['monthSummary'], ['categorySpending'], ['dailySpending'] — requires a documented invalidation map

alternatives.name: localStorage Persistence + Memoized Derived State (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: The absolute minimal-change approach for a greenfield, single-user, desktop-only app with no backend in scope. All 8 entities are stored directly in localStorage (keyed by entity type). On mount, data is read synchronously — no async fetch, no loading states. Derived aggregates (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via useMemo from the raw Expense + Category arrays and re-computed only when their dependencies change. No external caching library is introduced.
tradeoffs: Lowest blast radius of all alternatives: ~2–3 utility files (storage.ts, useExpenses.ts, useDerivedSummaries.ts) + consumer hooks; zero new dependencies
Synchronous localStorage.getItem() on mount is blocking but completes in <5ms for typical expense datasets (<1000 records), well within all performance budgets
localStorage is limited to ~5–10 MB per origin — sufficient for years of single-user expense data (avg expense record ~200 bytes → 25,000+ records before limit)
No network layer means no cache invalidation complexity — writes go directly to localStorage and trigger React state updates
useMemo for MonthSummary/CategorySpending/DailySpending avoids recomputation on unrelated state changes, keeping chart renders within 600ms
Zero resilience to localStorage being cleared (private browsing, user action) — data loss risk; no sync or backup (both out of scope, so acceptable)
Does not compose with a future real backend without a full refactor of the data layer — highest future migration cost of all alternatives
No optimistic update infrastructure — mutations are synchronous so this is not a practical limitation for the current scope

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full library adoption, all implementable without a backend (which is explicitly out of scope). Redis was excluded because it requires server-side infrastructure — incompatible with a frontend-only, desktop-web, greenfield scope. CDN caching was excluded because there is no static asset serving pattern for dynamic per-user expense data. IndexedDB was considered but excluded in favor of localStorage because the dataset size (single user, personal expenses) does not justify the async complexity of IndexedDB, and synchronous reads are a performance advantage for the sub-150ms micro-interaction constraint. The four selected alternatives cover: (1) pure client state management, (2) network-layer caching, (3) the React ecosystem standard for data-fetching, and (4) the absolute minimal-change path — giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit gradient to choose from.
structured.recommendation: alt-1-in-memory-zustand for immediate implementation, with alt-3-react-query-swr as the upgrade path if a real backend is introduced later. Zustand satisfies every performance constraint (synchronous reads, optimistic writes) with the lowest blast radius and a single small dependency. The localStorage persistence middleware gives durability without async complexity. If the scope ever expands to include a real API, the Zustand store can be replaced slice-by-slice with React Query queries — the component interfaces (custom hooks) remain identical, making the migration non-breaking.

alternatives.name: Browser LocalStorage with No Payment Provider
alternatives.description: Since CashPulse is explicitly scoped to visual/interaction design only (no backend API, no bank integration, no cloud sync), all expense data — including amounts, categories, budgets, and summaries — is persisted entirely in browser LocalStorage. No payment provider is integrated. The app operates as a fully client-side, offline-first tracker where users manually enter expenses. All 8 data entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) are serialized as JSON in LocalStorage. A thin data-access layer (e.g., a `storageService.ts` module) abstracts read/write operations.
tradeoffs: Zero external dependencies added — no SDK, no API keys, no network calls
Fully consistent with all scope-exclusion constraints (no backend, no bank integration, no cloud sync)
Data is device-local only — clearing browser storage destroys all data (acceptable given scope exclusions for backup/sync)
LocalStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB — sufficient for thousands of expense records but not unlimited
No payment processing capability whatsoever — if monetization is ever added, this must be replaced
Affects 1 new module: `storageService.ts`; touches all 8 entity read/write paths
references: CashPulse PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope
CashPulse PRD scope-exclusion: Bank account or credit card integration out of scope
CashPulse PRD scope-exclusion: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage out of scope

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (Client-Side, No Payment Provider)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are persisted in IndexedDB using Dexie.js as a lightweight wrapper. This replaces LocalStorage with a structured, queryable, async database that supports indexes (e.g., query expenses by date range, category, or amount). No payment provider is integrated — consistent with scope exclusions. Dexie.js adds 1 dependency (~22KB gzip). A `db.ts` module defines the schema and exposes typed async methods consumed by all screens. Enables efficient Insights screen queries (CategorySpending, DailySpending aggregations) without in-memory filtering.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: dexie (~22KB gzip) — manageable for a desktop-first app
Async API requires consistent use of async/await across all data-access paths — refactor risk if storageService was written synchronously
Supports structured queries (date ranges, category filters) natively — reduces complexity in Insights chart data preparation
Storage limit is effectively unbounded for practical expense tracking use (~hundreds of MB)
IndexedDB is supported in all target evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge latest 2 versions)
Affects 1 new module (`db.ts`) + all 7 screens that read/write entity data; no UI changes required
No payment processing capability — fully consistent with all scope exclusions
references: CashPulse PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope

alternatives.name: Mock REST API via MSW (Mock Service Worker) — Design-Fidelity Stub
alternatives.description: A realistic REST API contract is defined (OpenAPI spec) and intercepted entirely in the browser using Mock Service Worker (MSW). All 8 entities are served via mocked endpoints (GET /expenses, POST /expenses, GET /budgets, etc.) with realistic latency simulation (50–120ms). No real backend or payment provider exists. This approach future-proofs the frontend for a real API without violating scope exclusions, and allows the UI to be built against a stable contract. MSW intercepts fetch/XHR at the Service Worker level — zero production network traffic. Adds 2 dependencies: msw + a JSON fixture store.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: msw (~30KB dev) and a fixture/seed data module — both are dev/test dependencies only, zero production bundle impact if tree-shaken
Requires defining an OpenAPI or handler contract upfront — ~2–4 hours of schema work but pays dividends when a real backend is added
Realistic latency simulation stress-tests the 350ms screen transition and 600ms chart render performance constraints against async data flows
MSW handlers must be maintained in sync with UI expectations — drift risk if entity schemas evolve
Affects: 1 new `mocks/` directory with handlers + all 7 screens refactored to use fetch() instead of direct storage calls — medium blast radius
No payment processing capability — fully consistent with all scope exclusions
Enables Cypress/Playwright integration tests against realistic API responses without a live server
references: CashPilse PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope (MSW satisfies this — no real backend)

alternatives.name: Zustand In-Memory Store with Optional LocalStorage Persistence Plugin
alternatives.description: All application state for the 8 data entities is managed in a Zustand store — a lightweight (~1KB) React state manager. The `persist` middleware optionally serializes the store to LocalStorage automatically, providing persistence without a dedicated storage service. This collapses the data layer and state management into a single pattern: components read from and write to the Zustand store; persistence is a configuration concern, not an architectural one. No payment provider is integrated. Ideal for a greenfield desktop-first React app where the data model is relatively flat and the team wants minimal boilerplate.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: zustand (~1KB gzip) — extremely lightweight
Zustand `persist` middleware handles LocalStorage serialization automatically — eliminates need for a separate storageService module
In-memory store means all derived data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) can be computed as selectors — no separate aggregation queries needed
If LocalStorage persistence is enabled, same ~5MB storage cap applies as alt-local-storage-only
Store rehydration on page load must be handled carefully to avoid flash of empty state — requires a loading gate in the root component
Affects: 1 new `store/` directory + all 7 screens refactored to use Zustand hooks instead of local state — medium blast radius for a greenfield project (no migration cost)
No payment processing capability — fully consistent with all scope exclusions
Zustand devtools integration enables time-travel debugging for expense entry flows
references: CashPulse PRD scope-exclusion: Backend API development out of scope

structured.rationale: All four alternatives were selected because they are fully implementable within the hard constraints (no backend, no bank integration, no cloud sync — all explicit scope exclusions) and the greenfield nature of the project. Payment providers such as Stripe, Braintree, or PayPal were excluded entirely: CashPulse is a personal expense *tracker*, not a payment *processor* — it records expenses the user has already made, it does not initiate or process financial transactions. Integrating a payment SDK would violate the scope-exclusion constraints and introduce unnecessary PCI-DSS surface area. The four alternatives instead address the real architectural gap: how to store and serve the 8 data entities client-side in a way that supports the 7 screens and 25 features. The spectrum runs from minimal-change (LocalStorage only) through structured client-side DB (Dexie/IndexedDB), to API-contract-first (MSW), to unified state+persistence (Zustand). React Query and SWR were excluded because they are data-fetching libraries that presuppose a real or mock network layer — overkill without a backend contract defined first.
structured.recommendation: alt-zustand-in-memory — For a greenfield, desktop-first, single-user React app with no backend, Zustand with the persist middleware is the highest-leverage choice. It eliminates the need for a separate storage service module, collapses state management and persistence into one pattern, and keeps the bundle impact negligible (~1KB). The derived-data selector pattern maps cleanly onto the computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) without requiring async queries. If the project later graduates to a real backend, Zustand actions can be swapped to dispatch API calls with minimal UI-layer changes. The LocalStorage persistence cap is not a concern for a single-user personal tracker within the defined scope.

alternatives.name: In-App Toast / Snackbar Notification System (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Implement a lightweight, self-contained in-app notification layer using a React context + portal pattern. Toasts are rendered in a fixed overlay portal at the top-right of the viewport, managed by a global NotificationContext. Triggered by UI events (expense saved, budget threshold crossed, delete confirmed/reverted). No external dependencies — built entirely with existing React + CSS Modules/Tailwind stack. Covers: success confirmations, error states, the 3-second delete auto-revert countdown, and budget overage warnings. All toasts respect prefers-reduced-motion (instant appear/disappear instead of slide-in animation).
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses React context, useReducer, and ReactDOM.createPortal only
Blast radius: 3–5 files (NotificationContext.tsx, useNotification.ts, Toast.tsx, ToastContainer.tsx, plus call-sites in AddExpenseForm and ExpenseList)
Notifications are ephemeral and session-only — no persistence across page reloads
Cannot reach the user outside the browser tab (no push, no email) — acceptable given scope-exclusion constraint
Must manually wire toast triggers at every relevant call-site (form submit, delete confirm, budget check)
Fully WCAG 2.1 AA compliant: role='status' aria-live='polite' for success, role='alert' aria-live='assertive' for errors; focus management handled explicitly

alternatives.name: Radix UI Toast Primitive Integration
alternatives.description: Adopt the Radix UI Toast primitive (@radix-ui/react-toast) as the notification infrastructure. Radix provides a fully accessible, WAI-ARIA-compliant toast component with built-in focus trapping, keyboard dismissal (Escape), swipe-to-dismiss, and viewport management. Style it with existing design tokens (Inter font, blue-600 accent, 10px border-radius, card shadow). Wire to a thin ToastService singleton that any module can call. The 3-second delete auto-revert uses Radix's built-in duration prop with a visible countdown progress bar.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: @radix-ui/react-toast (~8KB gzipped); if Radix is already used elsewhere in the stack, this is zero net-new cost
Blast radius: 4–6 files (ToastProvider setup in App.tsx, ToastService.ts, styled Toast wrapper, call-sites in AddExpenseForm, ExpenseList, BudgetCard)
Radix handles all ARIA roles, aria-live regions, and keyboard interactions out of the box — reduces custom accessibility code by ~80%
Opinionated DOM structure may require CSS overrides to match design tokens precisely (card shadow, radius, Inter font)
Swipe-to-dismiss is touch-only and irrelevant for desktop-first scope, but harmless
Requires wrapping the app root in <ToastProvider> — one-time setup change to App.tsx

alternatives.name: Inline Contextual Feedback + Status Banner Pattern
alternatives.description: Forgo floating toasts entirely. Instead, surface all feedback inline and contextually: form submission results appear as a status banner directly below the form header; delete confirmation uses an inline undo row that replaces the deleted expense item for 3 seconds before collapsing; budget overage is shown as a persistent inline warning chip on the BudgetCard component. No global notification state — each component owns its own feedback state via local useState. This is the most architecturally minimal approach and aligns with the 'zero clutter' design language.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — pure React local state only
Blast radius: 2–4 files (AddExpenseForm.tsx gets inline status banner, ExpenseListItem.tsx gets inline undo row, BudgetCard.tsx gets overage chip) — no new shared infrastructure files needed
Feedback is always visible in context of the action — no risk of toasts being missed off-screen or auto-dismissed before the user reads them
Cannot surface cross-screen notifications (e.g., a budget warning while on the Insights screen) — acceptable given the 3-screen scope
Inline undo row requires careful layout design to avoid cumulative layout shift (CLS) — must reserve space or use CSS grid row insertion
Slightly more per-component complexity; each component manages its own feedback lifecycle independently

alternatives.name: Zustand-Backed Global Notification Store with Toast UI
alternatives.description: Use Zustand (a lightweight state manager, ~1.1KB) to create a global notificationStore that queues and manages notification state across all screens. The Toast UI layer reads from this store and renders via a portal. This enables cross-screen notifications (e.g., a budget threshold warning triggered on the Dashboard persists when navigating to Insights), deduplication logic, and a notification history accessible from a bell icon in the nav bar. Pairs well if Zustand is already used for expense/budget state management in the app.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency if Zustand is not already in the stack: zustand (~1.1KB gzipped); zero cost if already used for entity state

Blast radius: 6–9 files (notificationStore.ts, ToastContainer.tsx, Toast.tsx, NavBar.tsx updated with bell icon, NotificationHistory panel, plus call-sites across Dashboard, AddExpenseForm, InsightsScreen)
Enables notification history and cross-screen persistence — significant over-engineering for a scope-excluded push/email notification requirement
Bell icon + history panel adds UI surface area that may conflict with the 'zero clutter' design principle
Zustand store is synchronous and predictable — no async complexity; devtools integration available for debugging
WCAG compliance requires additional work: aria-live on the toast container, focus management for the history panel, keyboard-accessible bell icon (36×36px minimum)

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full design space from zero-infrastructure inline feedback to a cross-screen global notification store, all implementable within a greenfield React desktop web stack. Push notifications and email alerts are explicitly excluded by a hard scope-exclusion constraint, so no server-side or service-worker-based alternatives were considered. Browser Notification API was excluded because it requires user permission grants, is inappropriate for in-app transactional feedback, and conflicts with the desktop-first, single-session nature of CashPulse. Third-party notification SaaS (e.g., Novu, Knock) was excluded as gross over-engineering for a frontend-only PRD with no backend API. The four alternatives selected represent: (1) a pure custom build with zero deps, (2) a battle-tested accessible primitive with minimal deps, (3) a no-infrastructure inline pattern, and (4) a global store approach for completeness. All satisfy the hard WCAG 2.1 AA constraint and the soft 150ms micro-interaction performance constraint.
structured.recommendation: alt-03 (Inline Contextual Feedback) is the strongest fit for CashPulse's stated design philosophy of 'zero clutter' and its tightly scoped 3-screen surface area. It requires no new dependencies, has the lowest blast radius, and keeps feedback anchored to the action that triggered it — which is superior UX for a focused daily-use tool. If cross-screen notification needs emerge (e.g., budget warnings surfacing on Insights), alt-02 (Radix UI Toast) is the recommended upgrade path: it adds only one small dependency while delivering production-grade accessibility compliance out of the box, saving significant custom ARIA implementation effort.

alternatives.name: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: All expense data is loaded into the browser on initial page load and stored in a client-side state store (e.g., Zustand or React Context). Search and filter operations (by category, date range, amount, keyword) are performed synchronously using JavaScript array methods (.filter(), .sort()) directly on the in-memory dataset. No additional API calls are made during filtering. Pagination is handled via a windowed slice of the filtered array. This is the minimal-change approach — no new backend endpoints, no new dependencies beyond what a React state manager already provides.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies if a state manager (Zustand/Context) is already in use
Filtering latency is effectively 0ms for datasets under ~2,000 records, comfortably within the 150ms micro-interaction budget
Initial page load payload grows linearly with expense count — at 500 expenses (~50KB JSON) this is negligible, but degrades past ~5,000 records
No backend search endpoint needed, consistent with the scope-exclusion of backend API development
Affects ~3 modules: ExpenseList component, filter/search state slice, and the data-fetching hook
Stale data risk: filters always reflect the last full fetch; requires a manual refresh or polling strategy to stay current
Sorting and multi-field filtering logic must be maintained in the frontend codebase

alternatives.name: URL-Driven Query Parameters with Debounced Re-fetch
alternatives.description: Search and filter state is encoded directly into the URL as query parameters (e.g., ?category=food&dateFrom=2024-01-01&q=coffee). A debounced hook (300ms) watches for parameter changes and triggers a re-fetch of the filtered expense list from a local data layer (IndexedDB via Dexie.js or a mock/local REST layer). This approach makes filter state bookmarkable and shareable, supports browser back/forward navigation, and decouples UI state from component tree. Filtering logic lives in the data layer rather than component state.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: a URL state sync library (e.g., nuqs or React Router's useSearchParams) — minimal footprint
Optionally adds Dexie.js (~25KB gzipped) if IndexedDB is used as the local data layer for structured querying
Filter state survives page refresh and is deep-linkable — strong UX benefit for a desktop-first app
Debounce of 300ms on text search keeps within the 150ms micro-interaction budget for chip/category selections (which are instant) while preventing excessive re-renders on keystroke
Affects ~5 modules: ExpenseList, filter bar components, routing config, data-fetching hook, and URL sync utility
Requires discipline to keep URL params in sync with UI state — potential source of bugs if filter components are added later
IndexedDB querying adds complexity but enables true structured filtering (range queries on amount/date) without loading all records into memory

alternatives.name: Indexed Local Search with Fuse.js (Fuzzy Matching)
alternatives.description: Expense data is loaded into memory and indexed using Fuse.js, a lightweight fuzzy-search library. The index is built once on data load and rebuilt incrementally on mutations (add/edit/delete). Search queries run against the Fuse index for fuzzy text matching (merchant names, notes, categories), while structured filters (date range, amount range, category chips) are applied as post-index JavaScript predicates. This gives users a forgiving, Google-like search experience without a backend.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Fuse.js (~24KB minified, ~8KB gzipped) — small and well-maintained
Fuzzy matching significantly improves usability for frequent daily use (e.g., 'coffe' matches 'Coffee Shop') — aligns with the sub-15-second Quick Add UX goal
Index build time is ~5-15ms for 1,000 records — negligible, well within performance budgets
Search results include relevance scoring, enabling ranked display rather than simple chronological order
Affects ~4 modules: ExpenseList, search input component, data store/hook, and a new search-index utility module
Fuzzy matching can produce unexpected results for numeric amount searches — requires hybrid approach (exact match for amounts, fuzzy for text fields)
Index must be invalidated and rebuilt on every expense mutation — adds ~5ms overhead per add/edit/delete operation
No persistence of search index across sessions — rebuilt on every page load

alternatives.name: Structured Filter Engine with IndexedDB (Dexie.js)
alternatives.description: All expense data is persisted in IndexedDB via Dexie.js, which provides a structured, indexed local database with compound query support. Search and filtering are executed as Dexie queries against indexed fields (categoryId, date, amount). Text search is handled via a simple prefix-match index on merchant/description fields. This approach treats the browser as a full local database, enabling efficient range queries and pagination without loading all records into memory — the most scalable client-side option.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Dexie.js (~25KB gzipped) — requires schema definition and migration strategy from the outset
Enables true compound queries: e.g., 'all Food expenses in March over $20' execute as indexed DB queries, not full array scans
Scales to 50,000+ expense records without memory pressure — future-proof for power users
IndexedDB operations are async — requires careful loading state management to avoid UI flicker; must use skeleton loaders to stay within the 150ms perceived-interaction budget
Affects ~7 modules: data store, all CRUD hooks (expense add/edit/delete), ExpenseList, filter bar, a new db schema module, and migration utilities
Highest blast radius of all alternatives — touches the entire data layer and requires upfront schema design decisions
Text search is limited to prefix matching unless a separate inverted index is maintained manually; full fuzzy search requires combining with Fuse.js
Data lives only in the user's browser — no sync, consistent with scope exclusions, but users lose data on browser storage clear

structured.rationale: All four alternatives are scoped entirely to the frontend, consistent with the hard scope-exclusion of backend API development. The alternatives span a spectrum from zero-dependency in-memory filtering (alt-01) to a full local database engine (alt-04), giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/capability tradeoff axis. Server-side search solutions (Elasticsearch, Algolia, PostgreSQL full-text search) were excluded because backend development is explicitly out of scope. React Query's server-state model was excluded as a standalone option because there is no server to query. Web Workers for off-thread filtering were excluded as an alternative because the dataset sizes realistic for a personal expense tracker (under 5,000 records) do not justify the added complexity — the 150ms budget is easily met on the main thread. The recommendation leans toward alt-01 (client-side in-memory) as the pragmatic greenfield starting point, with alt-03 (Fuse.js) as a low-cost upgrade path if fuzzy UX becomes a priority.
structured.recommendation: alt-search-01 (Client-Side In-Memory Filtering) for the initial build. CashPulse is a personal expense tracker — a single user's realistic dataset (12–24 months of daily expenses) tops out around 1,500–3,000 records, well within the performance envelope of synchronous array filtering. This keeps the blast radius at 3 modules, adds zero new dependencies, and delivers sub-5ms filter response times that comfortably satisfy the 150ms micro-interaction constraint. If the product later prioritizes forgiving text search (e.g., fuzzy merchant name matching), layer in alt-search-03 (Fuse.js) as a targeted upgrade to the search input component only — it requires touching only 1 additional module beyond what alt-01 already establishes.

adrs.title: Client-Side Data Persistence via IndexedDB (Dexie.js)
adrs.decision: Use Dexie.js as the primary data persistence layer, storing all 8 entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, QuickAddSuggestion) in IndexedDB with typed repository wrappers. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie compound-index queries and memoized in React state. Dexie's liveQuery() provides reactive cross-screen updates.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a frontend-only, single-user desktop app with no backend in scope. Dexie.js (~24KB gzipped) provides structured, indexed queries essential for Insights screen aggregations (date ranges, category grouping), built-in schema versioning for safe entity evolution, and liveQuery() for reactive Dashboard updates after expense creation. localStorage (alt-1) lacks query capabilities and schema versioning. Zustand sessionStorage (alt-3) loses data on browser close. The hybrid approach (alt-4) adds unnecessary complexity for a single-user app. Dexie's async API is the only meaningful tradeoff, handled cleanly with loading guards.
alternatives: localStorage with JSON serialization — synchronous but no query language, no schema versioning
Zustand with sessionStorage — data lost on browser close, no structured queries
localStorage + Dexie hybrid — over-engineered two-tier architecture for single-user volumes

adrs.title: Styling via Tailwind CSS + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
adrs.decision: Use Tailwind CSS v3 with CVA (Class Variance Authority) and tailwind-merge as the styling system. All design tokens (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, typography, breakpoints) are codified in tailwind.config.js. Reusable UI primitives (Button, Card, Badge, Input, Chip) are defined with cva() typed variant APIs. No CSS-in-JS runtime.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse has a strict design token spec (7 radius values, 2 shadow tokens, 15 color tokens, 3 breakpoints, Inter font). Tailwind maps tokens directly to config with zero runtime overhead, protecting the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms transition performance budgets. CVA adds TypeScript-level variant enforcement, preventing token drift across 25 features and 7 screens. The prefers-reduced-motion constraint is handled natively via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant. styled-components (alt-styled-components) adds runtime CSS injection overhead. CSS Modules (alt-css-modules) lack built-in responsive variants and require duplicating breakpoints. Raw Tailwind without CVA (alt-tailwind) risks className string drift.
alternatives: CSS Modules + CSS Custom Properties — zero deps but no built-in responsive variants, breakpoint duplication
styled-components — runtime CSS injection risks 150ms budget on complex re-renders
Raw Tailwind without CVA — no TypeScript variant enforcement, className drift risk

adrs.title: Component Library: shadcn/ui (Radix UI + Tailwind)
adrs.decision: Use shadcn/ui components built on Radix UI headless primitives, styled with Tailwind CSS. Components are copied into the project (no runtime library lock-in). Radix provides accessible Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, and Toast primitives. Recharts is added separately for chart components on the Insights screen.
adrs.rationale: Radix UI primitives deliver WCAG 2.1 AA compliance (focus management, keyboard navigation, ARIA roles) out of the box — satisfying the hard accessibility constraint with minimal custom code. shadcn/ui's copy-paste model gives full design-token control via Tailwind config, unlike Mantine or MUI which require extensive theme overrides to match CashPulse's sharp financial design language. MUI's Material Design aesthetic bleeds through overrides, and its Emotion runtime adds bundle weight. Mantine's CSS Modules approach conflicts with the chosen Tailwind styling decision. The team owns all component source, eliminating version-lock risk.
alternatives: Mantine v7 — highest feature coverage but CSS Modules conflict with Tailwind decision, largest bundle
MUI v6 — Material Design aesthetic requires 30+ overrides, Emotion runtime overhead
Custom Radix + CSS Modules — highest dev effort, no pre-built component scaffolding

adrs.title: ORM/Data Access: Dexie.js Typed Repository Pattern
adrs.decision: Use Dexie.js as the data access layer with a typed repository pattern. One db.ts file defines the Dexie database schema with versioned migrations. Eight repository modules (one per entity) encapsulate CRUD and query logic. Derived entities are computed via Dexie's .where().between() and compound index queries. liveQuery() hooks provide reactive data binding to React components.
adrs.rationale: This decision is tightly coupled with ADR-001 (data persistence). Dexie.js replaces a traditional server-side ORM (Drizzle, Prisma) for this frontend-only app. Its typed Table<T> generics provide compile-time safety equivalent to Drizzle's schema-first approach. Zustand selectors (alt-02) lack query capabilities for Insights aggregations. RxDB (alt-03) adds 170KB of dependencies (RxDB + RxJS) for features (multi-tab sync, replication) that are out of scope. sql.js/Drizzle (alt-04) adds a 1.2MB WASM binary with cold-start latency.
alternatives: Zustand store with localStorage — no query language for aggregations
RxDB — 170KB bundle for unused sync/replication features
sql.js + Drizzle ORM — 1.2MB WASM binary, cold-start latency

adrs.title: Caching: Zustand In-Memory Store with Dexie Persistence
adrs.decision: Use Zustand as the in-memory cache layer on top of Dexie.js persistence. On app load, hot data (current month expenses, active budget, user settings, categories) is hydrated from Dexie into Zustand. Subsequent reads are synchronous from the Zustand store. Writes go through Zustand (optimistic update) then persist to Dexie (durable). Derived data (MonthSummary, CategorySpending) is computed as Zustand selectors with useShallow to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
adrs.rationale: This hybrid approach gives the best of both worlds: Dexie provides durable, queryable persistence (ADR-001/004) while Zustand provides synchronous reads that guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions. Pure Dexie reads are async and would require loading states on every interaction. Pure Zustand with localStorage persistence lacks the query capabilities needed for Insights. HTTP cache headers (alt-2) and React Query (alt-3) presuppose a network layer that doesn't exist. The Zustand store is ~3KB gzipped and likely already needed for UI state management.
alternatives: Dexie-only reads — async overhead requires loading states on every interaction
HTTP Cache + Service Worker — no network layer exists
React Query — presupposes API endpoints that don't exist
localStorage + useMemo — no structured queries for Insights

adrs.title: No Payment Provider Required
adrs.decision: CashPulse does not integrate any payment provider. The app is a personal expense tracker that records expenses the user has already made — it does not initiate or process financial transactions. All expense data (amounts, categories, payment methods) is user-entered and stored client-side via Dexie.js/Zustand.
adrs.rationale: The PRD explicitly scopes out bank account integration, automatic transaction import, and backend API development. CashPulse records past expenses, not future payments. Integrating Stripe, Braintree, or any payment SDK would violate scope exclusions and introduce unnecessary PCI-DSS surface area. The 'payment method' field (Cash, Debit Card, Credit Card, UPI/Other) is a user-selected label for categorization, not a payment processing integration.
alternatives: Stripe/Braintree integration — violates scope exclusions, unnecessary PCI-DSS surface
Mock API via MSW — adds complexity without value for a tracker app

adrs.title: Notification: Inline Contextual Feedback + Radix Toast
adrs.decision: Use inline contextual feedback as the primary notification pattern (status banners below form headers, inline undo rows for delete confirmation). Supplement with Radix UI Toast (@radix-ui/react-toast) for cross-context notifications (currency change toast, export confirmation). Toasts render in a fixed portal at bottom-center per the PRD spec. All notifications respect prefers-reduced-motion.
adrs.rationale: The PRD specifies inline feedback patterns: Save button shows '✓ Saved!' inline, Delete uses inline 'Confirm delete?' with 3-second auto-revert, and currency change shows a bottom-center toast. Inline feedback (alt-03) matches the 'zero clutter' design philosophy and keeps feedback anchored to actions. Radix Toast (alt-02) is added for the specific toast patterns the PRD requires (currency change, CSV export) — it's already in the dependency tree via shadcn/ui's Radix primitives. A full Zustand notification store (alt-04) with bell icon over-engineers beyond PRD requirements.
alternatives: Custom toast context — reinvents Radix Toast's accessibility features
Zustand notification store with bell icon — over-engineered, conflicts with zero-clutter design
Inline-only with no toast — cannot implement the PRD's currency change toast requirement

adrs.title: Search: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering
adrs.decision: Implement search and filtering via synchronous JavaScript array methods (.filter(), .sort(), .reduce()) on the Zustand in-memory store. Category filter dropdown on Dashboard filters the expense list. Insights aggregations are computed as Zustand selectors. No external search library is added initially. Fuse.js is the documented upgrade path if fuzzy text search becomes a priority.
adrs.rationale: A single user's realistic dataset (12-24 months of daily expenses) is 1,500-3,000 records — well within synchronous array filtering performance. Filter response times are sub-5ms, far under the 150ms micro-interaction budget. The PRD's search feature (feat-024) is 'could-have' priority, not must-have. Adding Fuse.js or Dexie compound queries for the initial build is premature. The Zustand store already holds all expense data in memory (ADR-005), making array filtering zero-cost. URL-driven query params (alt-02) add routing complexity without clear UX benefit for a 3-screen app.
alternatives: Fuse.js fuzzy search — documented upgrade path, adds 8KB for fuzzy matching
URL-driven query params — routing complexity without UX benefit for 3 screens
Dexie.js structured queries — async overhead for simple category filtering

decisions.rationale: Dexie.js (IndexedDB) provides structured queries with indexes for Insights aggregations, built-in schema versioning for safe entity evolution, and liveQuery() for reactive Dashboard updates. The 24KB bundle is negligible. localStorage lacks query capabilities and schema versioning. Zustand sessionStorage loses data on browser close. The hybrid approach is over-engineered for single-user volumes.

Tailwind CSS + CVA provides zero-runtime styling with TypeScript-level variant enforcement for CashPulse's strict 15-color, 7-radius, 2-shadow design token spec. CVA's typed variant API prevents token drift across 25 features. Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant natively handles prefers-reduced-motion. The 5-package dependency cost is justified by the design-system-grade token enforcement. styled-components adds runtime overhead; CSS Modules lack responsive variants.

shadcn/ui delivers Radix UI's battle-tested WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility (hard constraint) with full Tailwind design-token control. Components are project-owned (no version lock). Radix primitives provide Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, and Toast — all needed by CashPulse. Mantine's CSS Modules conflict with the Tailwind decision.
