Establishes a formal API contract (OpenAPI-style route definitions) that future backend developers can implement directly
Network interception adds ~1-5ms overhead per request — well within 150ms micro-interaction budget
Requires a service worker registration step (msw/browser) — adds complexity to app bootstrap
All data access becomes async by default — consistent with real-world patterns but requires async handling in all 8 entity stores
MSW must be excluded from production builds via environment flags — adds build configuration overhead
Affects 10-12 modules: 8 entity stores, 1 MSW handler file, 1 service worker registration, 1 API client utility
Blast radius is medium-high due to cross-cutting async refactor, but future migration cost to real backend drops to near-zero

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) with Origin Private File System
alternatives.description: Run a full SQLite database in the browser using sql.js (SQLite compiled to WebAssembly). The database file is persisted to the browser's Origin Private File System (OPFS) via the File System Access API. This gives CashPulse a real relational database with full SQL query support, foreign key constraints, and complex aggregations — ideal for the MonthSummary, CategorySpending, and DailySpending computed entities on the Insights screen.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 large dependency: sql.js WASM binary (~1.2MB uncompressed, ~400KB gzipped) — significant initial load cost
OPFS requires a SharedArrayBuffer context (COOP/COEP headers) — requires server-side header configuration even for static hosting
Full SQL support enables complex GROUP BY aggregations for Insights charts in a single query — reduces JS computation
WASM initialization takes 200-500ms on first load — may conflict with the 350ms screen transition constraint if not pre-initialized
Browser support: Chrome 86+, Firefox 111+, Safari 15.2+ — compatible with 'latest 2 versions' constraint but OPFS persistence has uneven Safari support
Requires a dedicated worker thread to avoid blocking the main thread — adds 1 worker file and a postMessage communication layer
Affects 9-11 modules: 8 entity stores, 1 db worker, 1 OPFS persistence manager, 1 schema migration file
Overkill for a personal expense tracker with thousands (not millions) of records — sql.js is best justified only if complex reporting queries become a bottleneck
references: MDN Web Docs: Origin Private File System — https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/File_System_API/Origin_private_file_system
Chrome Developers: Storage for the web — https://developer.chrome.com/docs/apps/offline_storage/

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full relational database, all implementable client-side without a backend (respecting the hard scope-exclusion constraint). Payment provider integration in the traditional sense (Stripe, Braintree, etc.) is explicitly out of scope — CashPulse tracks expenses but does not process payments. The gap is therefore reframed as the data persistence and API integration strategy. Options excluded: (1) Firebase/Supabase — violates the 'no cloud sync/backend' scope exclusion and adds auth complexity. (2) Redux Persist alone — redundant with Zustand's built-in persist middleware, adds unnecessary abstraction. (3) PouchDB/CouchDB sync — sync capability is explicitly out of scope. The four selected alternatives represent: minimal change (LocalStorage), structured upgrade (IndexedDB/Dexie), future-proof contract (MSW), and maximum capability (SQLite WASM).
structured.recommendation: alt-indexeddb — Dexie.js over IndexedDB is the optimal choice for CashPulse. It adds only 1 dependency, provides sufficient storage for any realistic personal expense dataset, enables indexed queries that keep the Insights screen chart renders within the 600ms constraint, and supports schema versioning for future entity changes. It avoids the complexity of MSW's service worker setup and the WASM initialization latency of sql.js, while being a meaningful upgrade over LocalStorage's 5MB cap and lack of query indexing. The async API aligns with modern React patterns and keeps all 8 entity stores consistent in their data access model.

alternatives.name: In-App Toast / Snackbar Notifications Only
alternatives.description: Deliver all user feedback (expense saved, budget threshold crossed, delete confirmed/reverted) exclusively through a lightweight in-app toast/snackbar system rendered in a fixed portal at the bottom-right of the viewport. No external channels. A single ToastProvider context manages a queue of messages with auto-dismiss timers (3–5s). Toasts carry severity levels (info, success, warning, error) expressed via icon + text label — never color alone — satisfying WCAG AA. The 3-second delete auto-revert safety window is surfaced as an 'Undo' action button inside the toast. Reduced-motion users receive instant-appear/instant-dismiss toasts with no slide or fade animation.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies — implementable with React context + a small custom hook (~150 lines)
Blast radius limited to 3 files: ToastProvider.tsx, useToast.ts, Toast.tsx (new), plus call-sites in ExpenseForm and DeleteConfirmation components
No persistence — if the user navigates away or refreshes, in-flight toasts are lost
Cannot surface budget threshold alerts unless the user is actively using the app (acceptable given scope-exclusion of push/email notifications)
Must carefully manage z-index layering against modals and popovers to avoid occlusion
Undo action inside toast requires precise 3-second countdown timer logic to meet the data-integrity constraint
references: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1 — Keyboard (Escape to dismiss toast)
WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.3 — Status Messages (role='status' or role='alert')
React Portal API for overlay rendering: https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/createPortal

alternatives.name: Inline Contextual Banners + Status Chips
alternatives.description: Surface all feedback inline within the screen where the action occurred, rather than in a floating overlay. Expense saved → a green-bordered success banner appears at the top of the Add Expense card. Budget exceeded → a warning banner with an amber left-border stripe appears at the top of the Dashboard budget card. Delete confirmation → the expense row transforms into a 3-second countdown chip with an Undo button in place of the row itself. All banners use icon + text (never color alone). Banners are dismissed via an explicit close button (36×36px minimum) or auto-dismiss after 5s. No external dependencies; implemented as a BannerSlot component injected into each screen's layout.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — pure React component additions (~200 lines across 3 new components)

Blast radius: 5–7 files — BannerSlot.tsx (new), InlineBanner.tsx (new), CountdownChip.tsx (new), Dashboard.tsx (modified), AddExpenseForm.tsx (modified), ExpenseListItem.tsx (modified), layout CSS tokens (modified)
Highly contextual — users immediately see feedback adjacent to the action, reducing cognitive load
Requires layout space reservation in each screen to avoid content jump when banners appear/disappear (use min-height or CSS grid row reservation)
Layout shift during banner entry/exit must complete within 250ms transition budget
Does not support cross-screen notifications (e.g., a budget alert surfaced while on the Insights screen)
references: Nielsen Norman Group — Inline Validation and Feedback: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/errors-forms-design-guidelines/
CSS Grid implicit row animation for layout-shift-free banner insertion: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/grid-template-rows

alternatives.name: Toast System + Persistent Notification Badge (Hybrid)
alternatives.description: Combine the minimal toast system (alt-toast-only) with a persistent notification bell icon in the top navigation bar that accumulates unread budget-threshold alerts as a badge count. Clicking the bell opens a Notification Popover listing recent alerts (e.g., 'Dining budget 90% used') with timestamps. Alerts are generated client-side by a NotificationEngine service that evaluates CategorySpending and Budget entities after each expense mutation. Toasts handle transient feedback (save, delete/undo); the badge+popover handles persistent, reviewable alerts. All badge counts use aria-label ('3 unread notifications'). Popover is keyboard-navigable and dismissible via Escape.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new components (NotificationBell.tsx, NotificationPopover.tsx) and 1 new service (notificationEngine.ts) — approximately 300 lines total
Blast radius: 6–8 files — above 3 new files, plus AppShell/NavBar.tsx (modified), Budget entity read logic (modified), CategorySpending query hook (modified), and shared z-index/token CSS (modified)
Notifications are ephemeral (in-memory / localStorage) — lost on hard refresh unless persisted to localStorage (~50 extra lines); no backend required
localStorage persistence adds a soft dependency on browser storage availability (private browsing may clear it)
Popover open/close must meet the 150ms micro-interaction budget — requires careful avoidance of layout-triggering CSS properties
Most complete UX for budget awareness without violating the push/email scope exclusion
Requires defining threshold rules (e.g., 80%, 90%, 100%) as part of UserSettings entity — minor data model touch
references: WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.3 — Status Messages (aria-live for badge updates)
ARIA Authoring Practices — Dialog (Modal) pattern for popover: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/dialog-modal/
Headless UI Popover for accessible popover primitives: https://headlessui.com/react/popover

alternatives.name: Web Notifications API (Browser Push — Client-Side Only)
alternatives.description: Use the browser's native Notification API to fire OS-level desktop notifications when budget thresholds are crossed, even when the CashPulse tab is backgrounded. No server-side push infrastructure — notifications are triggered purely client-side by the NotificationEngine after expense mutations. Requires a one-time permission prompt. Falls back gracefully to in-app toasts if permission is denied or the API is unsupported. Notification payloads include category name, percentage used, and a deep-link action back to the Insights screen.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new service (browserNotificationService.ts, ~80 lines) and a permission-request UI component (~60 lines) — minimal code footprint

Blast radius: 4–5 files — browserNotificationService.ts (new), PermissionPrompt.tsx (new), notificationEngine.ts (new or shared with alt-toast-plus-badge), UserSettings entity (modified to store permission state), AppShell.tsx (modified for permission prompt trigger)

Directly conflicts with the hard scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Push notifications or email alerts for budget thresholds' — this alternative is included for completeness but is EXCLUDED by constraints
Browser Notification API requires HTTPS in production
Permission prompt UX is browser-controlled and cannot be styled to match the design system
Safari support for Web Notifications on macOS requires Safari 16+ and is inconsistent on iOS (out of scope anyway)
Users frequently deny notification permissions, making this an unreliable primary channel
references: Can I Use — Notifications API: https://caniuse.com/notifications
Constraint: scope-exclusion — 'Out of scope: Push notifications or email alerts for budget thresholds'

structured.recommendation: alt-toast-plus-badge — The hybrid Toast + Notification Badge approach best balances the project's needs. Toasts handle the critical transient feedback loop (expense saved, delete/undo countdown) that directly supports the 'completable in under 15 seconds' usability constraint, while the persistent badge+popover gives budget-aware users a reviewable alert history without requiring any backend or violating scope exclusions. The blast radius is contained to 6–8 files, all implementable in the existing React stack with zero new runtime dependencies. The 150ms popover interaction budget is achievable with CSS transform-based animations. alt-browser-notification-api is explicitly excluded by the hard scope constraint. alt-inline-banner is a strong runner-up for its contextual clarity but falls short for cross-screen budget alerts.
structured.rationale: Alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from minimal-change (alt-toast-only) to contextual (alt-inline-banner) to hybrid persistent (alt-toast-plus-badge) to native OS-level (alt-browser-notification-api). The Web Notifications API alternative was included despite being scope-excluded to document the decision explicitly and prevent future re-exploration. Server-side channels (email, SMS, WebSocket push) were excluded entirely because: (1) the project is scoped to visual/interaction design only with no backend API development, (2) there is no authentication system, and (3) email/push alerts are explicitly out of scope. A dedicated 'notification center' screen was excluded as overkill for a single-user personal tracker with no persistence layer.

alternatives.name: Client-Side In-Memory Filtering (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: All expense data is loaded once into memory on app initialization (or per-screen mount) and filtered/sorted entirely on the client using native JavaScript array methods (Array.filter, Array.sort, String.includes). A single React state slice holds the active filter criteria (date range, category IDs, amount bounds, keyword). A useMemo hook re-derives the visible expense list whenever criteria or the raw dataset changes. No additional network requests are made for search or filter operations. Pagination is handled via a local slice index. This approach is viable given the personal-use scope: a single user's expense history is unlikely to exceed 5,000–10,000 records, which JavaScript can filter in <5ms.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses only existing React state and memoization primitives
All filter/search interactions complete well under the 150ms micro-interaction budget (typically <10ms for ≤10k records)
Initial data fetch payload grows linearly with history size; may become noticeable (>500KB JSON) after 2–3 years of daily use
No server-side query optimization — full dataset must be resident in memory at all times
Affects ~3 modules: ExpenseListView, useExpenseStore (state slice), filterUtils.ts (new utility file)
Sorting and filtering logic must be duplicated if a future server-side API is introduced

alternatives.name: IndexedDB-Backed Local Query Layer (Fuse.js + idb)
alternatives.description: Expenses are persisted to IndexedDB via the `idb` wrapper library and queried using indexed lookups for structured filters (date, category, amount) combined with Fuse.js for fuzzy full-text search on description/merchant fields. On app load, a lightweight metadata index is hydrated; full records are fetched on demand. Filter criteria are translated into IDBKeyRange queries for date/amount bounds and category ID lookups, with Fuse.js applied as a post-filter pass on the result set. This mirrors a lightweight local database pattern and scales to tens of thousands of records without memory pressure.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new runtime dependencies: `idb` (~2KB gzipped) and `fuse.js` (~8KB gzipped)
IndexedDB setup requires a schema migration strategy — adds a db-migrations.ts module and version management overhead
Fuzzy search via Fuse.js adds subjective relevance tuning (threshold, keys weighting) that must be calibrated for financial data
Structured filter queries (date range, category) are fast (<2ms via IDBKeyRange); fuzzy text search on large sets adds 20–80ms depending on corpus size
Affects ~6 modules: db/schema.ts (new), db/migrations.ts (new), useExpenseStore, ExpenseListView, filterUtils.ts (new), SearchBar component
Requires async data access patterns throughout — components must handle loading states for filter results
Offline-first by design, which aligns with the greenfield desktop-web scope

alternatives.name: URL-Driven Filter State with React Query + REST Stubs
alternatives.description: Filter and search criteria are encoded as URL query parameters (e.g., ?category=food&from=2024-01-01&q=coffee) and managed via a URL state library (e.g., nuqs or native URLSearchParams). React Query handles data fetching against a local mock/stub API layer (MSW or json-server) that accepts these query params and returns filtered, paginated results. This architecture is forward-compatible with a real backend API and makes filter state shareable/bookmarkable. The stub layer can be replaced with a real endpoint without changing any component code.
tradeoffs: Adds 3 dependencies: `@tanstack/react-query`, `msw` (dev) or `json-server` (dev), and optionally `nuqs` for URL state (~5KB gzipped)
URL state encoding adds complexity for multi-value filters (e.g., multiple selected categories require array serialization)
MSW service worker setup requires a public/mockServiceWorker.js file and browser registration step
React Query's caching layer means filter changes may show stale data for up to the configured staleTime — requires tuning to 0ms for instant filter feedback
Affects ~8 modules: queryClient.ts (new), mocks/handlers.ts (new), useExpenseQuery hook (new), ExpenseListView, FilterPanel, SearchBar, router config, and public/ directory
Deeplink-friendly: filter state survives page refresh and can be shared — a usability benefit for power users
Highest migration compatibility: swapping MSW for a real API requires only changing the base URL, not component logic

alternatives.name: Zustand Store with Derived Selector Filtering
alternatives.description: A Zustand global store holds the canonical expense array and a separate filterCriteria slice. Filtered results are exposed as computed selectors using Zustand's built-in selector memoization (with shallow equality). Category chips, date pickers, and the search input all dispatch actions to update filterCriteria; the derived selector re-runs only when criteria or the expense array changes. This is a middle path: no new async layer, no IndexedDB complexity, but with a structured, testable state architecture that cleanly separates raw data from view-layer filter state.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: `zustand` (~3KB gzipped) — likely already planned for global state given the 8-entity data model
Selector memoization is shallow by default; complex filter objects require custom equality functions to avoid unnecessary re-renders
All data remains in memory — same payload size concern as alt-1 for very long-term users (2+ years of daily entries)
Filter state is not URL-encoded — browser back/forward does not restore filter context, and filters reset on page refresh
Affects ~4 modules: store/expenseStore.ts (new), store/filterSlice.ts (new), ExpenseListView, FilterPanel
Highly testable: filter logic is pure functions operating on plain arrays, easily unit-tested without DOM or async setup
Consistent with React's unidirectional data flow; no prop drilling required for deeply nested filter controls

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency client filtering to a mock-API-backed query layer, covering the realistic implementation space for a greenfield, desktop-web-only, single-user personal finance app. Server-side search (Elasticsearch, Algolia, PostgreSQL full-text) was excluded because the scope explicitly rules out backend API development. React Server Components / server actions were excluded as they require a Node.js runtime not implied by the stack. SQLite via WASM was considered but excluded as overly complex relative to the data volume a single user generates. The four selected alternatives each represent a meaningfully different architectural commitment: pure client memory (alt-1), local database (alt-2), API-contract-first (alt-3), and structured global state (alt-4).
structured.recommendation: alt-4 (Zustand Store with Derived Selector Filtering). For CashPulse's greenfield, single-user, desktop-web scope, Zustand is the pragmatic sweet spot. It adds only one small dependency that is almost certainly needed anyway for the 8-entity data model, keeps all filter logic as pure synchronous functions (guaranteeing sub-150ms micro-interaction performance), and produces a clean, testable architecture without the async complexity of IndexedDB or the mock-server overhead of alt-3. If URL-shareable filters become a future requirement, nuqs can be layered on top of the Zustand slice without restructuring the store. Alt-1 is acceptable if Zustand is not adopted globally, but alt-4's explicit filter slice makes the codebase significantly more maintainable as the feature set grows to 25 features.

adrs.title: Client-Side Data Persistence via IndexedDB (Dexie.js)
adrs.decision: Use Dexie.js as the primary persistence layer for all durable entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion). Dexie tables are indexed on date, categoryId, and amount fields. Derived/computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are NOT stored in IndexedDB but computed on demand and cached in the Zustand UI state layer.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a fully client-side, single-user desktop web app with no backend (hard scope exclusion). Dexie.js provides structured, indexed storage with a ~47KB gzipped footprint, versioned schema migrations, and sub-millisecond indexed reads that comfortably satisfy the 150ms micro-interaction budget. localStorage (alt-1 in gap-data-store) was rejected due to its 5MB cap and lack of query indexing — the Insights screen's aggregation queries would require full array scans. SQLite/WASM (alt-3) was rejected for its 1.3MB WASM bundle and 200-800ms initialization latency that conflicts with the 350ms screen transition constraint. Supabase (alt-4) directly violates the scope exclusion of cloud storage and backend API development.
alternatives: alt-1: localStorage + in-memory state — rejected due to 5MB cap and no indexed queries
alt-3: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) — rejected due to 1.3MB bundle and WASM init latency
alt-4: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — rejected as it violates scope exclusions (no backend, no cloud sync)

adrs.title: Styling via Tailwind CSS + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
adrs.decision: Use Tailwind CSS v3 with a custom tailwind.config.js encoding all CashPulse design tokens (Inter font, 4px base unit, 16px/10px/20px/8px radius scale, card/elevated shadows, full color palette). Use Class Variance Authority (CVA) for typed component variant definitions (Button, Badge, Chip, Input, Card). Dynamic per-category colors use CSS custom properties or inline style as a contained exception.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse has a strict, well-defined design token set with 5+ component variant axes. Tailwind's theme extension makes it structurally impossible to use off-token values without a config change — critical for a financial app where visual consistency signals trust. CVA adds typed variant schemas that eliminate verbose conditional className logic across 7 screens and 20+ components. Both produce static CSS/class strings with zero runtime overhead, keeping micro-interactions well within 150ms. Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant handles the prefers-reduced-motion constraint declaratively. CSS Modules (alt-css-modules) were rejected because they lack structural token enforcement — drift is possible across 27+ module files. styled-components (alt-styled-components) were rejected due to 12KB runtime overhead and CSS-in-JS hydration complexity.
alternatives: alt-css-modules: CSS Modules — rejected due to lack of structural token enforcement
alt-tailwind: Plain Tailwind without CVA — rejected due to verbose variant logic for multi-variant components
alt-styled-components: styled-components — rejected due to runtime overhead and SSR complexity

adrs.title: Component Library: shadcn/ui (Radix UI + Tailwind)
adrs.decision: Use shadcn/ui as the component foundation. Radix UI primitives provide WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA roles for Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip, and DropdownMenu. Components are vendored into the project for full control. Recharts is used for chart primitives (bar, line, donut). react-number-format is used for the amount input's decimal auto-formatting.
adrs.rationale: shadcn/ui delivers the optimal intersection for CashPulse: Radix primitives handle all accessibility-critical interactive patterns (Settings Dialog, Expense Detail Popover, Category Filter Dropdown, Date Picker) with zero custom ARIA work. Vendored components mean no runtime library overhead and no upgrade-migration risk. Tailwind integration is native, so the design token system flows through without fighting library defaults. Mantine (alt-mantine) was the strong runner-up but was rejected due to its ~250KB effective bundle size and the risk of visual drift from incomplete theme overrides. MUI (alt-mui) was rejected for its 300KB+ bundle, Material Design aesthetic requiring 8-12 hours of theme override work, and Emotion runtime overhead. Custom headless (alt-custom-headless) was rejected due to 3-5 additional dev-days for hand-building all form inputs, cards, and layout components.
alternatives: alt-mantine: Mantine v7 — rejected due to bundle size (~250KB) and theme override risk
alt-mui: Material UI v6 — rejected due to bundle size (300KB+), Material aesthetic mismatch, Emotion runtime
alt-custom-headless: Custom on Radix only — rejected due to high upfront dev cost (3-5 extra days)

adrs.title: Data Access: Dexie.js + Zustand Hybrid Architecture
adrs.decision: Adopt a two-layer data architecture: Dexie.js is the source of truth for durable entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion). Zustand holds derived/computed views (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) and ephemeral UI state (active filters, selected month, modal open state). dexie-react-hooks' useLiveQuery bridges the two layers reactively. Writes go to Dexie first, then trigger Zustand derived-state recomputation.
adrs.rationale: The hybrid split is natural for CashPulse's 8 entities: 5 are durable write-heavy records, 3 are derived aggregates that feed the Dashboard and Insights screens. Dexie's indexed reads are sub-millisecond for typical expense volumes, satisfying the 150ms micro-interaction budget. Zustand's memoized selectors cache derived aggregates, eliminating recalculation on every navigation and supporting the 600ms chart render target. Pure Zustand+localStorage (alt-02) was rejected due to the 5MB localStorage cap and full-array-scan performance for aggregations. PGlite+Drizzle (alt-04) was rejected for its 3MB WASM bundle and 200-800ms initialization latency. Pure Dexie (alt-01) was acceptable but lacks the reactive UI state management that Zustand provides for filter state, selected month, and modal controls.
alternatives: alt-01: Dexie.js only — acceptable but lacks reactive UI state management
alt-02: Zustand + localStorage — rejected due to 5MB cap and no indexed queries
alt-04: PGlite + Drizzle ORM — rejected due to 3MB WASM bundle and init latency

adrs.title: Caching: Zustand In-Memory Store with Memoized Derived Selectors
adrs.decision: Use Zustand as the in-memory cache layer. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed from Dexie data on app load and after each expense mutation, then cached in Zustand slices. Selectors use shallow equality checks to prevent unnecessary re-renders. No TTL mechanism is needed because the single-user, single-tab model means the cache is always fresh — invalidation happens synchronously on mutation.
adrs.rationale: For a client-side app with no network layer, the caching problem reduces to memoization of expensive computations. Zustand's synchronous reads guarantee sub-150ms micro-interactions. The localStorage-backed TTL cache (alt-2 in gap-caching-strategy) was rejected as unnecessary complexity — TTL is meaningful when data can change externally, but CashPulse has a single writer (the user in the current tab). Service Worker caching (alt-3) is overkill with no backend to intercept. TanStack Query (alt-4) adds 13KB of query framework overhead to wrap what are fundamentally synchronous local reads.
alternatives: alt-2: localStorage-backed cache with TTL — rejected as unnecessary for single-writer model
alt-3: Service Worker + Workbox — rejected as overkill with no backend
alt-4: TanStack Query + localStorage persistence — rejected due to overhead for local-only reads

adrs.title: No Payment Provider — Client-Side Expense Tracking Only
adrs.decision: CashPulse does not integrate any payment provider. The app is a pure expense tracker — users manually log expenses. Data persistence uses Dexie.js (IndexedDB) as decided in ADR-001/ADR-004. The gap-payment-provider gap is resolved as a data persistence decision, not a payment integration decision.
adrs.rationale: The PRD explicitly scopes CashPulse to visual and interaction design with no real money movement. Bank account integration, automatic transaction import, and payment processing are all listed as out of scope. The Dexie.js/IndexedDB persistence strategy (alt-indexeddb in gap-payment-provider) provides indexed queries for the Insights screen within the 600ms chart render constraint, schema versioning for future entity changes, and storage capacity far exceeding localStorage's 5MB cap. MSW mock API (alt-mock-rest-api) was rejected as unnecessary indirection for a client-only app. SQLite WASM (alt-sqlite-wasm) was rejected for its 1.2MB bundle and COOP/COEP header requirements.
alternatives: alt-local-storage: Browser localStorage — rejected due to 5MB cap and no indexed queries
alt-mock-rest-api: MSW mock REST API — rejected as unnecessary indirection
alt-sqlite-wasm: SQLite via sql.js WASM — rejected due to bundle size and header requirements

adrs.title: Notification Strategy: In-App Toast System
adrs.decision: Use a lightweight in-app toast/snackbar system for all transient user feedback (expense saved, currency changed, delete confirmation). Toasts render in a fixed portal at the bottom-center of the viewport with auto-dismiss timers (2-5s). Toasts carry severity levels (info, success, warning, error) expressed via icon + text label — never color alone. The 3-second delete auto-revert uses an Undo action button inside the toast. No persistent notification badge or bell icon in v1.
adrs.rationale: The PRD specifies exactly two notification patterns: (1) a toast for currency change ('Currency updated to EUR', 2s auto-dismiss) and (2) inline delete confirmation with 3-second auto-revert. The toast-only approach (alt-toast-only) covers both patterns with zero new dependencies and a blast radius of only 3 files. The hybrid toast+badge approach (alt-toast-plus-badge) was rejected as over-engineered for v1 — the PRD does not specify a notification bell or persistent alert history. Browser Notifications API (alt-browser-notification-api) is explicitly excluded by the scope constraint ('Out of scope: Push notifications or email alerts'). Inline banners (alt-inline-banner) were rejected because the PRD specifically calls for a toast at bottom-center for currency changes.
alternatives: alt-toast-plus-badge: Toast + notification badge — rejected as over-engineered for v1 scope
alt-inline-banner: Inline contextual banners — rejected; PRD specifies toast for currency change
alt-browser-notification-api: Web Notifications API — rejected; explicitly out of scope

adrs.title: Search and Filtering: Zustand Derived Selector Filtering
adrs.decision: Implement expense filtering via a Zustand filterCriteria slice with derived selectors. Category filter dropdown on Dashboard dispatches actions to update filterCriteria. Filtered results are exposed as computed selectors using shallow equality memoization. Search (feat-024, could-have) would layer on top of this same slice if implemented. All filter operations are synchronous in-memory array operations.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse's must-have filtering is limited to the category filter dropdown on the Dashboard expense list. A single user's expense history is unlikely to exceed 10,000 records, which JavaScript can filter in <5ms — well within the 150ms micro-interaction budget. Zustand is already adopted for UI state (ADR-004/ADR-005), so adding a filterCriteria slice is a natural extension with minimal blast radius (~4 files). IndexedDB-backed Fuse.js (alt-2) was rejected as over-engineered for a single category dropdown filter. URL-driven React Query (alt-3) was rejected due to mock-server overhead and 3 additional dependencies. Pure client-side filtering (alt-1) is functionally equivalent but lacks the structured state management that Zustand provides.
alternatives: alt-1: Pure client-side Array.filter — acceptable but less structured
alt-2: IndexedDB + Fuse.js — rejected as over-engineered for category filtering
alt-3: URL-driven React Query + MSW — rejected due to mock-server overhead

decisions.rationale: Dexie.js (IndexedDB) provides structured, indexed storage with sub-millisecond reads, versioned migrations, and effectively unlimited storage — the sweet spot for a client-side expense tracker. localStorage's 5MB cap and lack of indexing make it a false economy given the Insights screen's aggregation needs. SQLite WASM's 1.3MB bundle and init latency conflict with performance constraints. Supabase violates scope exclusions.

Tailwind CSS + CVA structurally enforces CashPulse's strict design token set via tailwind.config.js while CVA provides typed variant schemas for the 5+ multi-variant component types (Button, Badge, Chip, Input, Card). Zero runtime overhead. motion-reduce: variant handles prefers-reduced-motion declaratively. The one workaround (per-category dynamic colors via inline styles) is contained to the Category chip component.

shadcn/ui delivers Radix UI accessibility primitives (Dialog, Popover, Select, Tooltip) with zero runtime overhead via vendored components. Tailwind integration is native, so the design token system flows through without fighting library defaults. Recharts fills the charting gap. react-number-format fills the amount input gap. Mantine's bundle size and MUI's Material aesthetic mismatch make them suboptimal for CashPulse's sharp financial design language.

The Dexie.js + Zustand hybrid naturally splits CashPulse's 8 entities: Dexie owns 5 durable write-heavy entities with indexed queries; Zustand owns 3 derived/computed views and ephemeral UI state. dexie-react-hooks bridges the layers reactively. This satisfies the 150ms interaction budget via indexed reads and the 600ms chart render target via memoized derived selectors. The two-layer contract is well-documented in local-first web app patterns.

For a single-user client-side app with no network layer, caching reduces to memoization. Zustand's synchronous in-memory store with memoized selectors guarantees sub-150ms reads. TTL-based caching is unnecessary because the single writer (current user) means the cache is always fresh — invalidation is synchronous on mutation. Service Worker and TanStack Query add complexity without benefit for local-only data.

CashPulse is a pure expense tracker with no real money movement — payment provider integration is out of scope. The gap resolves to data persistence strategy, where Dexie.js/IndexedDB provides indexed queries for Insights screen performance, schema versioning, and storage capacity far exceeding localStorage. This aligns with the data-store and ORM decisions (ADR-001, ADR-004).

The PRD specifies exactly two notification patterns: a bottom-center toast for currency change (2s auto-dismiss) and inline delete confirmation with 3-second auto-revert. The toast-only approach covers both with zero dependencies and minimal blast radius (3 files). The hybrid toast+badge is over-engineered for v1. Browser Notifications API is explicitly out of scope. Inline banners conflict with the PRD's specified toast placement.

CashPulse's must-have filtering is a single category dropdown on the Dashboard. Zustand is already adopted for UI state, so a filterCriteria slice is a natural, low-blast-radius extension (~4 files). Synchronous in-memory filtering of <10k records completes in <5ms, well within the 150ms budget. IndexedDB+Fuse.js and URL-driven React Query are over-engineered for this scope.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie.js + Zustand Hybrid Data Access
implementationPatterns.rule: All durable entities (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion) MUST be read and written through Dexie.js table operations. Derived/computed entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) MUST be computed from Dexie data and cached in Zustand slices. Zustand is the read cache; Dexie is the source of truth. On every write to Dexie, the corresponding Zustand derived slice MUST be recomputed and updated.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-orm-strategy decision (ADR-004). The two-layer contract prevents stale-data bugs: Dexie owns persistence, Zustand owns reactive UI state. This satisfies the 150ms micro-interaction budget via indexed Dexie reads and memoized Zustand selectors.
forbids: Direct localStorage reads/writes for entity data
Storing derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) in Dexie tables

implementationPatterns.title: Tailwind via Design Tokens Only — CashPulse Override
implementationPatterns.rule: All spacing, color, radius, shadow, and typography values MUST come from the tailwind.config.js theme extension. Arbitrary values (e.g., `w-[137px]`) are forbidden except for the three PRD-specified fixed widths: max-w-[520px] (Add Expense), max-w-[720px] (Insights), and w-[360px] (Dashboard left column). Per-category dynamic colors MUST use CSS custom properties (--category-color) set via inline style, consumed by Tailwind's arbitrary property syntax `bg-[var(--category-color)]`.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (ADR-002). Structural token enforcement via tailwind.config.js prevents design drift across 7 screens and 20+ components. The three layout-width exceptions are PRD-specified fixed dimensions that don't map to the 4px spacing scale.
forbids: Arbitrary Tailwind values except the three PRD-specified layout widths
Hardcoded hex colors in className strings
Using Tailwind's default spacing scale instead of the 4px-based custom scale

implementationPatterns.title: CVA for Component Variant Definitions
implementationPatterns.rule: Every component with more than one visual variant (Button, Badge, Chip, Input, Card) MUST define its variants using a cva() call with typed variant props. Variant keys MUST map to design-system concepts (intent, size, status), not implementation details. Default variants MUST be specified. Components MUST export their variant props type via VariantProps<typeof componentVariants>.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach decision (ADR-002). CVA provides typed, declarative variant management that eliminates verbose conditional className logic and ensures consistent variant application across all 7 screens.
forbids: Inline conditional className ternaries for variant logic in component JSX

implementationPatterns.title: Zod Validation at Data Boundaries
implementationPatterns.rule: All data entering the persistence layer (Dexie writes) MUST be validated through a Zod schema. Each durable entity (Expense, Category, Budget, UserSettings, QuickAddSuggestion) MUST have a corresponding Zod schema in a shared schemas/ directory. Form submission handlers MUST call schema.parse() before calling the Dexie write function. Validation errors MUST be caught and surfaced as inline form errors, not thrown to the global error boundary.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-orm-strategy and gap-data-store decisions (ADR-001, ADR-004). Without a backend, the Dexie write boundary is the only enforcement point for data integrity constraints (amount max $99,999.99, 2 decimal places, non-future dates, 100-char note limit). Zod provides runtime type safety with excellent TypeScript inference.
forbids: Writing to Dexie tables without prior Zod validation
Using TypeScript type assertions (as Expense) instead of runtime validation at write boundaries

implementationPatterns.title: Toast-Based Error and Feedback Envelope
implementationPatterns.rule: All user-facing feedback (success, warning, error) MUST be delivered through the ToastProvider context. Toast messages MUST include a severity level (success | warning | error | info), a human-readable message string, and an optional action (e.g., Undo). Toasts MUST use role='status' for informational messages and role='alert' for errors. Toasts MUST auto-dismiss: success after 2s, info after 3s, warning after 5s, error after 5s. The delete confirmation pattern uses a toast with an Undo action and a 3-second countdown.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-notification-channel decision (ADR-007). The PRD specifies toast notifications for currency change (2s) and delete confirmation (3s auto-revert). A consistent toast envelope prevents ad-hoc alert() calls and ensures WCAG 4.1.3 (Status Messages) compliance via ARIA roles.
forbids: Using window.alert() or window.confirm() for user feedback
Rendering error messages outside the toast system for non-form errors

implementationPatterns.title: Focus Rings and Reduced Motion
implementationPatterns.rule: All interactive elements MUST have a visible focus indicator: focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-primary-600 focus-visible:ring-offset-2. All animations MUST be wrapped in motion-safe: Tailwind variants. Chart animations MUST check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') and render instantly if true. Click targets MUST be at least 36×36px (min-w-9 min-h-9 in the custom spacing scale).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-003 (hard accessibility constraint) and constraint-nfr-004. WCAG 2.1 AA requires visible focus indicators and respect for reduced motion preferences. Tailwind's motion-safe:/motion-reduce: variants make this declarative rather than imperative.
forbids: Animations without motion-safe: guard or equivalent runtime check
Interactive elements smaller than 36×36px

implementationPatterns.title: Zustand Slice Pattern for UI State
implementationPatterns.rule: Zustand store MUST be organized as independent slices: filterSlice (active category filter, selected month), uiSlice (modal open state, active tab, popover target), derivedSlice (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending caches). Each slice MUST be defined in its own file under store/. Selectors MUST use shallow equality comparison to prevent unnecessary re-renders. Components MUST select only the specific state they need, never the entire store.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-search-strategy (ADR-008) and gap-caching-strategy (ADR-005). The slice pattern keeps state concerns separated and testable. Shallow selectors prevent the performance degradation that would violate the 150ms micro-interaction budget.
forbids: Selecting the entire Zustand store object in a component
Storing Dexie-persisted entity data in Zustand (use useLiveQuery instead)

implementationPatterns.title: Structured Console Logging for Client-Side App
implementationPatterns.rule: All error and warning logs MUST use a shared logger utility that outputs structured JSON-like objects to console.error/console.warn with consistent fields: { context: string, action: string, error?: Error, metadata?: Record<string, unknown> }. Debug logs MUST be gated behind a DEBUG flag (import.meta.env.DEV). Production builds MUST NOT include debug-level logs. Dexie transaction failures MUST be logged with the entity type and operation as context.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from the baseline logging-structured-pino pattern, adapted for a client-side app with no server. Pino is not applicable (no Node.js runtime), but structured logging discipline is still critical for debugging Dexie transaction failures, Zod validation errors, and derived-state computation issues.
forbids: Bare console.log() calls in production code
Unstructured string-only error logging

implementationPatterns.title: No Authentication — Single-User Client-Side App
implementationPatterns.rule: CashPulse MUST NOT implement any authentication, authorization, or session management. All data is device-local in IndexedDB. There are no user accounts, no login screens, no JWT tokens, no middleware guards. The app loads directly to the Dashboard screen. If multi-user support is added in the future, this decision must be revisited via a new ADR.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from the scope exclusion constraint (constraint-scope-1: 'Out of scope: User authentication and account management') and the resolved gap (gap-auth-strategy: resolvedValue='none'). The baseline auth-middleware-required pattern is explicitly overridden for this project.
forbids: Auth middleware, guards, or session checks
User ID fields on data entities
Login/signup screens or routes

implementationPatterns.title: Recharts with ARIA Labels and Motion Control
implementationPatterns.rule: All chart components (BarChart, LineChart, PieChart/donut) MUST use Recharts via the shadcn/ui chart wrapper. Every chart container MUST have role='img' and a descriptive aria-label summarizing the data (e.g., 'Bar chart showing daily spending for March 2026, averaging $41.58 per day'). Chart animations MUST respect prefers-reduced-motion by setting isAnimationActive={!prefersReducedMotion}. Tooltips MUST be keyboard-accessible via Tab navigation through data points.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-component-library decision (ADR-003) and constraint-nfr-003 (WCAG 2.1 AA). The PRD explicitly requires role='img' with aria-label on charts and keyboard-accessible tooltips. Recharts is the charting library bundled with shadcn/ui's chart component.
forbids: Charts without role='img' and aria-label
Chart animations without reduced-motion check

stackConfig.backend: None (client-side only — no backend per scope exclusion)
stackConfig.styling: Tailwind CSS v3 + Class Variance Authority (CVA)
stackConfig.componentLibrary: shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives + Tailwind) + Recharts + react-number-format

additions.description: Returns the budget summary for the currently selected month. Response: { month: string (YYYY-MM), totalSpent: number, monthlyLimit: number, remaining: number, currency: string, status: 'ON_TRACK' | 'HEADS_UP' | 'OVER_BUDGET', spendPercentage: number }. Used by feat-001 Budget Summary Dashboard card. Returns 200 with empty/zero values when no expenses exist (feat-017). Returns 404 if no budget record exists for the month (falls back to defaultMonthlyBudget from UserSettings).

Lists all Budget records. Query params: none. Response: { data: Array<{ id: string, month: string, monthlyLimit: number, currency: string, createdAt: string }> }. Used by Settings dialog (feat-014) to display and manage monthly budgets.

Creates or upserts a Budget record for a given month. Request body: { month: string (YYYY-MM), monthlyLimit: number (>0), currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY' }. Response 201: { id: string, month: string, monthlyLimit: number, currency: string, createdAt: string }. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope if monthlyLimit <= 0 or month format invalid.

Updates the Budget record for a specific month (YYYY-MM). Request body: { monthlyLimit: number (>0), currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY' }. Response 200: updated Budget object. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for invalid input, 404 if no budget for that month.

Returns a paginated, filterable list of Expense records. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required), categoryId (uuid, optional), page (integer, default 1), pageSize (integer, default 20), sortBy ('date'|'amount', default 'date'), sortOrder ('asc'|'desc', default 'desc'). Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, amount, description, categoryId, date, paymentMethod, note, createdAt, updatedAt, category: { id, name, color, icon } }>, total: number, page: number, pageSize: number }. Used by feat-003 Recent Expenses List and feat-010 Biggest Expenses List (sortBy=amount). Returns empty array for no-data state (feat-017).

Returns a single Expense record by UUID. Response 200: { id, amount, description, categoryId, date, paymentMethod, note, createdAt, updatedAt, category: { id, name, color, icon } }. Errors: 404 ErrorEnvelope if not found. Used by feat-004 Expense Detail Popover.

Creates a new Expense record. Request body: { amount: number (>0), description: string (max 255), categoryId: uuid, date: string (ISO date, no future dates), paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Response 201: full Expense object with nested category. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for validation failures (amount <= 0, future date, missing required fields). On success, triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, and QuickAddSuggestion records. Used by feat-005 Add/Edit Expense Form.

Updates an existing Expense record. Request body: { amount?: number (>0), description?: string (max 255), categoryId?: uuid, date?: string (ISO date, no future dates), paymentMethod?: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100) }. Response 200: updated Expense object with nested category. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for validation failures, 404 if not found. Triggers recomputation of derived entities. Used by feat-005 Edit flow.

Deletes an Expense record by UUID. Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. Errors: 404 ErrorEnvelope if not found. Triggers recomputation of MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending, and QuickAddSuggestion. Used by feat-004 Expense Detail Popover delete with inline confirmation.

Full-text search across expenses by description, note, or amount. Query params: q (string, required, min 1 char), month (YYYY-MM, optional — omit to search all months), page (integer, default 1), pageSize (integer, default 20). Response 200: { data: Array<Expense with nested category>, total: number, page: number, pageSize: number }. Used by feat-024 Search Expenses. Returns empty array when no matches.

Returns the top N expenses by amount for a given month. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required), limit (integer, default 5, max 20). Response 200: { data: Array<{ rank: number, id, amount, description, categoryId, date, paymentMethod, category: { id, name, color, icon } }> }. Used by feat-010 Biggest Expenses List. Returns empty array for no-data state.

Returns all Category records sorted by sortOrder. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, name, color, icon, isDefault, sortOrder, createdAt }> }. Used by feat-002 donut chart legend, feat-003 filter dropdown, feat-005 category selector grid, feat-009 top categories, feat-014 Settings category manager.

Creates a new user-defined Category. Request body: { name: string (max 50, unique), color: string (hex, e.g. #3B82F6), icon: string (max 50) }. Response 201: { id, name, color, icon, isDefault: false, sortOrder: number, createdAt }. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope if name is duplicate or color format invalid. Used by feat-015 Inline Category Creation.

Updates an existing Category. Request body: { name?: string (max 50), color?: string (hex), icon?: string (max 50), sortOrder?: number }. Response 200: updated Category object. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for validation failures, 403 ErrorEnvelope if attempting to mutate a default category's name/icon, 404 if not found. Used by feat-014 Settings category manager.

Deletes a user-created Category. Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. Errors: 403 ErrorEnvelope if category isDefault=true (system categories cannot be deleted), 404 if not found, 400 ErrorEnvelope if category has associated expenses (must reassign first). Used by feat-014 Settings category manager trash icon.

Returns the computed MonthSummary for a given month (YYYY-MM). Response 200: { id, month, totalSpent, transactionCount, dailyAverage, budgetLimit, budgetStatus: 'ON_TRACK'|'HEADS_UP'|'OVER_BUDGET', remainingAmount, computedAt }. Returns zero-value summary for months with no expenses (feat-017). Used by feat-007 Spending Insights Period Summary stat cells.

Returns MonthSummary records for two consecutive months for comparison. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required — the 'current' month; previous month is derived automatically). Response 200: { current: MonthSummary, previous: MonthSummary, difference: number, differenceDirection: 'less'|'more'|'same' }. Used by feat-011 Month-over-Month Comparison card and feat-007 trend indicators.

Returns CategorySpending records for all categories in a given month (YYYY-MM). Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, categoryId, month, totalAmount, percentage, transactionCount, computedAt, category: { id, name, color, icon } }> } sorted by totalAmount descending. Used by feat-002 Donut Chart, feat-009 Top Categories Breakdown. Returns empty array for no-data state (feat-017).

Returns DailySpending records for every day in a given month (YYYY-MM). Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, date, totalAmount, transactionCount, computedAt }>, monthlyAverage: number }. Days with no spending are included with totalAmount: 0 and transactionCount: 0 to support continuous chart rendering. Used by feat-008 Daily Spending Chart. Returns all-zero array for no-data state.

Returns the top QuickAddSuggestion records ranked by frequency then recency. Query params: limit (integer, default 4, max 10). Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, description, amount, categoryId, paymentMethod, frequency, lastUsedAt, category: { id, name, color, icon } }> }. Returns empty array when no frequent expenses exist (feat-017 — hides Quick Add section). Used by feat-006 Quick Entry Suggestions.

Returns the singleton UserSettings record. Response 200: { id, currency: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget: number, updatedAt: string }. Used by feat-014 Settings Dialog and feat-022 Multi-Currency Support.

Updates the singleton UserSettings record. Request body: { currency?: 'USD'|'EUR'|'GBP'|'INR'|'JPY', defaultMonthlyBudget?: number (>0) }. Response 200: updated UserSettings object { id, currency, defaultMonthlyBudget, updatedAt }. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for invalid currency code or non-positive budget. Currency change triggers toast notification (feat-018). Used by feat-014 Settings Dialog.

Exports expense data as a CSV file download. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, optional — omit for all-time export), categoryId (uuid, optional filter). Response 200: Content-Type text/csv with Content-Disposition attachment; filename='cashpulse-expenses-{month}.csv'. CSV columns: date, description, category, amount, paymentMethod, note. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for invalid query params. Used by feat-016 Export CSV button on Insights screen.

Returns per-category budget limits for a given month. Query params: month (YYYY-MM, required). Response 200: { data: Array<{ categoryId, month, limit: number, totalSpent: number, remaining: number, percentage: number, status: 'ON_TRACK'|'HEADS_UP'|'OVER_BUDGET', category: { id, name, color, icon } }> }. Used by feat-025 Per-Category Budget Limits. Returns empty array if no per-category budgets set.

Creates or upserts a per-category budget limit. Request body: { categoryId: uuid, month: string (YYYY-MM), limit: number (>0) }. Response 201: { categoryId, month, limit, createdAt }. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope if limit <= 0 or categoryId not found. Used by feat-025 Per-Category Budget Limits settings.

Removes a per-category budget limit for a specific category and month. Response 200: { success: true }. Errors: 404 ErrorEnvelope if no matching record. Used by feat-025 Per-Category Budget Limits management.

Returns all saved recurring expense templates. Response 200: { data: Array<{ id, description, amount, categoryId, paymentMethod, note, intervalDays: number, nextDueDate: string, createdAt, category: { id, name, color, icon } }> }. Used by feat-023 Recurring Expense Templates.

Creates a new recurring expense template. Request body: { description: string (max 255), amount: number (>0), categoryId: uuid, paymentMethod: 'CASH'|'DEBIT_CARD'|'CREDIT_CARD'|'UPI_OTHER', note?: string (max 100), intervalDays: number (>0), nextDueDate: string (ISO date) }. Response 201: created template object. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope for validation failures. Used by feat-023.
Updates a recurring expense template. Request body: same optional fields as POST. Response 200: updated template object. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope, 404 if not found. Used by feat-023.
Deletes a recurring expense template. Response 200: { success: true, deletedId: string }. Errors: 404 ErrorEnvelope if not found. Used by feat-023.

Instantly logs an expense from a recurring template (auto-log action). Request body: { date?: string (ISO date, defaults to today, no future dates) }. Response 201: created Expense object. Errors: 400 ErrorEnvelope if date is in the future, 404 if template not found. Triggers recomputation of derived entities. Used by feat-023.

navigationTargets.trigger: expense-row: row click (opens expense detail popover)
date-picker-input: input field click (opens calendar dropdown)
export-csv-button: click triggers GET /api/export/csv download (stays on screen)
popover-actions: Delete confirmed (DELETE /api/expenses/{id} success, closes popover)

structured.themeStrategy: Single light-mode theme only. All tokens are defined as Tailwind theme extensions in tailwind.config.js. Per-category dynamic colors bypass the token system via CSS custom properties (--category-color) set through inline style on each category-scoped element, consumed via Tailwind's arbitrary property syntax (e.g., bg-[var(--category-color)]). No dark-mode or brand-switching variants are required for this scope; the token set is structured semantically (color.brand.*, color.surface.*, color.status.*, color.chart.*, color.text.*, color.overlay.*, space.*, typography.size.*, typography.weight.*, radius.*, shadow.*) so a dark-mode layer can be added later by overriding leaf values without renaming tokens.

alternatives.name: localStorage + in-memory state (Zustand/Context)
alternatives.description: All 8 data entities are stored as serialized JSON in the browser's localStorage. A lightweight state manager (Zustand or React Context + useReducer) holds the working in-memory copy, syncing to localStorage on every mutation. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed on-the-fly via selector functions rather than stored separately. QuickAddSuggestion is built from a frequency map over Expense records.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies if using React Context; +1 dependency (zustand ~3KB gzipped) for Zustand variant
localStorage cap of ~5MB limits history to roughly 50,000–100,000 expense records — more than sufficient for a single-user personal tracker
No query language: filtering/aggregation for Insights charts must be hand-written JS — adds ~200–400 LOC of utility functions
Data is not portable across browsers/devices (scope-exclusion: no sync/cloud — acceptable per constraints)
Synchronous localStorage.setItem can block the main thread on large payloads; mitigated by debouncing writes or using a Web Worker
Blast radius: affects ~3 files (store initializer, persistence middleware, entity selectors) — lowest blast radius of all options
Chart renders stay well within 600ms since all data is already in memory — no async fetch latency

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: All 8 entities are stored in a structured IndexedDB database managed by Dexie.js. Each entity maps to a Dexie table with typed indexes (e.g., Expense indexed on date, categoryId; Budget indexed on categoryId). Derived summary entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via Dexie's Collection API and cached in React state. Dexie's liveQuery() hook enables reactive UI updates without manual cache invalidation.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: dexie (~23KB gzipped) — no backend or build-time tooling changes required
Supports storage of hundreds of thousands of records with indexed queries — future-proof for power users
Async API (Promise-based) adds complexity to data-fetching layer; all reads must be awaited, requiring loading states in 7 screens
liveQuery() reactivity simplifies Dashboard and Insights refresh but requires wrapping components in useLiveQuery — affects ~10–15 component files
IndexedDB is supported in all target evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) — no compatibility risk
Migration path: Dexie's versioned schema migrations handle entity changes cleanly (e.g., adding fields to Expense)
Blast radius: medium — affects store layer (~5 files), all data-fetching hooks (~8 hooks), and loading-state UI in screens (~7 screens)

alternatives.name: SQLite via sql.js (WASM) + localStorage persistence
alternatives.description: sql.js compiles SQLite to WebAssembly, running a full relational database in-browser. All 8 entities are modeled as normalized SQL tables with foreign keys and indexes. The database binary is serialized to localStorage (or a File System Access API file) on each write. Derived entities (MonthSummary, CategorySpending, DailySpending) are computed via SQL GROUP BY queries, making Insights aggregations trivial to implement correctly.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: sql.js (~1.5MB WASM binary) — significantly increases initial bundle size and load time; requires async WASM initialization before any data access
WASM load adds ~300–800ms cold-start latency on first page load, potentially conflicting with the 350ms screen-transition constraint on initial render
Full SQL expressiveness makes complex Insights queries (e.g., rolling 30-day averages, category % breakdowns) trivial — 10–20 LOC vs 200+ LOC in alt-1
Serializing the entire DB binary to localStorage on every write is expensive for large datasets; requires debouncing or a Web Worker to avoid main-thread jank
Overkill for the 8 entities and single-user scope defined in this PRD — engineering complexity outweighs query convenience
Blast radius: high — affects build config (WASM asset handling), store layer, all query hooks, and requires async initialization guard across all 7 screens

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — client-only, anonymous/local mode
alternatives.description: Supabase provides a hosted PostgreSQL backend with a typed JS client. All 8 entities are modeled as Postgres tables with Row Level Security. The Supabase JS client handles all CRUD operations. Since auth and multi-user are out of scope, a single anonymous session or a fixed local user ID is used. Supabase Realtime can power live Dashboard updates. This option is included as the 'maximum capability' alternative.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~75KB gzipped) plus a required Supabase project (external service dependency)
Violates the soft scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Data backup, sync, or cloud storage' — data would live in a remote Postgres instance
Violates the soft scope-exclusion constraint: 'Out of scope: Backend API development' — requires Supabase project setup, table migrations, and RLS policy configuration
Network latency for every read/write risks breaching the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen-transition performance constraints without aggressive client-side caching
Requires internet connectivity — app becomes non-functional offline, which is a poor UX for a daily-use expense tracker
Blast radius: critical — affects build config, environment variables, all data hooks, error handling for network failures, and introduces an external service dependency with its own SLA

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency in-browser storage to a fully hosted cloud database, covering every realistic option for a greenfield, desktop-web-only, single-user personal finance app. PostgreSQL standalone (without Supabase) was excluded because it requires a backend server, which directly violates the hard scope-exclusion on backend API development. Firebase/Firestore was excluded for the same reason (cloud sync out of scope) and because it adds more complexity than Supabase without meaningful benefit here. A pure in-memory (no persistence) option was excluded because it would lose all data on page refresh, making the app unusable as a daily tracker. The four selected alternatives represent: (1) the minimal-change, zero-infrastructure path; (2) the structured browser-native path with real query capabilities; (3) the full-SQL-in-browser path for completeness; and (4) the cloud path to explicitly surface its constraint violations.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 (IndexedDB via Dexie.js) is the recommended choice. It provides structured, indexed storage that scales gracefully with real user data, supports all 8 entities with proper relationships and efficient queries, and keeps everything client-side (respecting all scope-exclusions around backend, sync, and auth). Dexie's liveQuery() eliminates manual cache invalidation across the Dashboard and Insights screens, and its async API is a natural fit for React's data-fetching patterns. The single 23KB dependency is a proportionate cost. alt-1 (localStorage) is a valid fallback if the team wants absolute zero dependencies and is comfortable writing manual aggregation logic for the Insights charts — it should be chosen if the Insights feature set is simple (e.g., only current-month totals, no rolling windows).

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS v3 (Utility-First)
alternatives.description: Use Tailwind CSS as the sole styling layer. Design tokens (spacing, radii, shadows, colors) are codified in tailwind.config.js under a custom theme extension. All components — cards, chips, popovers, form inputs, badges — are styled via utility classes directly in JSX. The 4px base spacing unit maps to Tailwind's default scale (space-1 = 4px). Custom values for card-radius (16px → rounded-2xl), button-radius (10px → rounded-[10px]), badge-radius (20px → rounded-full), and input-radius (8px → rounded-lg) are added to the theme. Focus rings are enforced via ring-2 ring-blue-600 ring-offset-2 utilities. The prefers-reduced-motion constraint is handled via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant prefix on all transition/animation utilities.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero runtime CSS-in-JS overhead — all styles are statically extracted at build time, directly supporting the 150ms micro-interaction and 350ms screen transition performance budgets
POSITIVE: Tailwind's JIT engine produces a minimal CSS bundle (~5–15KB gzipped for a project this size), keeping parse/render time negligible
POSITIVE: motion-reduce: variant natively handles the prefers-reduced-motion soft constraint with no extra code
POSITIVE: Responsive breakpoint utilities (md:, lg:, xl:) map cleanly to the three required breakpoints (768px, 1024px, 1440px)
NEGATIVE: Adds 1 new dependency (tailwindcss + postcss + autoprefixer — 3 dev deps total); requires PostCSS pipeline configuration
NEGATIVE: Custom token values (10px button radius, 20px badge radius, specific shadow values) require tailwind.config.js entries — any token drift between config and design must be manually audited
NEGATIVE: Long className strings on complex components (e.g., the Add Expense form with validation states) reduce JSX readability; mitigated by extracting component abstractions
NEGATIVE: WCAG 2.1 AA focus ring enforcement (2px blue-600, 2px offset) must be applied consistently by convention — no compile-time enforcement; requires team discipline or a lint rule
NEGATIVE: Inline conditional styling for validation states (e.g., error borders on amount input) requires string interpolation or a utility like clsx/cva — adds 1–2 additional micro-dependencies

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal Change / Zero Runtime)
alternatives.description: Use CSS Modules with standard CSS (or optionally PostCSS for nesting). Each component gets a co-located .module.css file. Design tokens are defined once in a global :root block (tokens.css) as CSS custom properties (--radius-card: 16px; --color-primary: #2563eb; --shadow-card: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.06); etc.) and consumed via var() in module files. Focus rings, WCAG targets, and reduced-motion rules live in a global base.css. This is the most framework-agnostic, lowest-dependency approach — supported natively by Vite, Next.js, and Create React App without additional configuration.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Zero additional runtime dependencies — CSS Modules are natively supported by all major React bundlers (Vite, Next.js, CRA) out of the box
POSITIVE: CSS custom properties for tokens provide a single source of truth; token changes in tokens.css propagate everywhere instantly with no rebuild required for dev
POSITIVE: Scoped class names eliminate all style collision risk across the 7 screens and shared component library
POSITIVE: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) blocks in base.css or per-module files handle the motion constraint cleanly with standard CSS
POSITIVE: Lowest blast radius of all options — adopting this approach touches only the build config (0 changes) and introduces a file-per-component pattern

NEGATIVE: Verbose for highly dynamic styles — the Add Expense form's real-time validation states (error/success borders, focus rings) require multiple class compositions via composes: or clsx, adding boilerplate
NEGATIVE: No built-in utility for responsive variants — breakpoint media queries must be manually written in each module file, increasing per-component CSS line count by ~30% vs Tailwind

NEGATIVE: Design token enforcement is purely by convention; a developer can hardcode #2563eb instead of var(--color-primary) with no tooling warning unless a stylelint rule is configured (adds 1 dev dep)
NEGATIVE: Theming or future dark mode (currently out of scope but a common follow-on) requires re-architecting token structure
NEGATIVE: Slightly higher cognitive overhead when tracing styles — must navigate between .jsx and .module.css files; tooling like CSS Modules TypeScript types (adds 1 dev dep) helps

alternatives.name: styled-components v6 (CSS-in-JS)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components to co-locate styles with component logic. A ThemeProvider at the app root exposes all design tokens (colors, spacing scale, radii, shadows, typography) as a typed theme object. Each component (Card, Button, Input, Badge, Chip) is a styled primitive. Dynamic styles for validation states, focus rings, and interaction states are expressed as interpolated props (e.g., ${props => props.$hasError && 'border-color: var(--color-error)'}). The createGlobalStyle API handles base resets, Inter font loading, and the prefers-reduced-motion media query globally.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Strongest co-location of logic and styles — component props drive visual states (error, disabled, selected) with full TypeScript type safety on the theme object
POSITIVE: ThemeProvider enforces token usage at the component API level — impossible to use an off-token color without explicitly bypassing the theme
POSITIVE: Excellent for the design system's dynamic states: chip selection, popover open/close, budget status colors all map naturally to prop-driven style interpolations

NEGATIVE: Adds 1 production dependency (styled-components ~12KB gzipped) with a runtime CSS injection cost — styles are generated and injected at component render time, creating a measurable overhead risk against the 150ms micro-interaction budget on lower-end hardware
NEGATIVE: styled-components v6 requires React 18+ and does not support React Server Components (RSC) — if the project ever moves to Next.js App Router, a full migration would be required

NEGATIVE: CSS-in-JS runtime means no static CSS extraction by default — critical path CSS is not available until JS executes, slightly increasing Time to First Meaningful Paint vs CSS Modules or Tailwind
NEGATIVE: Larger learning curve for developers unfamiliar with the tagged template literal API; interpolation bugs (missing semicolons, prop naming collisions) can be subtle
NEGATIVE: Bundle size impact: styled-components adds ~12KB gzipped to the production bundle — acceptable for this app size but non-trivial
NEGATIVE: Debugging requires styled-components Babel plugin (1 additional dev dep) to get readable class names in DevTools; without it, generated class names are hashes

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS + CVA (Class Variance Authority) — Structured Utility-First
alternatives.description: Extends alt-tailwind with Class Variance Authority (CVA) as a variant management layer. CVA defines typed component variant APIs (e.g., button({ intent: 'primary', size: 'md' })) that resolve to Tailwind class strings. This directly addresses Tailwind's readability weakness for complex multi-state components like the Add Expense form, category chips, and budget status badges. Tailwind handles all base styling and responsive utilities; CVA handles the combinatorial variant logic (validation states × focus states × disabled states). The tailwind-merge utility (1 dep) resolves class conflicts when variants are composed. This is the approach used by shadcn/ui and is well-established in the React/Tailwind ecosystem.
tradeoffs: POSITIVE: Retains all performance benefits of Tailwind (static extraction, zero runtime, JIT) while adding structured variant management
POSITIVE: CVA produces fully typed variant APIs — TypeScript will error if an invalid variant combination is passed to a component, enforcing design system consistency at compile time
POSITIVE: Directly solves the WCAG focus ring consistency problem: focus ring utilities are baked into the CVA base definition for each interactive component, not applied ad-hoc
POSITIVE: Scales well across all 7 screens and 25 features — the variant system grows linearly with component complexity rather than exponentially
POSITIVE: shadcn/ui components (if adopted) use this exact pattern, providing a ready-made accessible component library aligned with the design tokens
NEGATIVE: Adds 2 additional dev/prod dependencies beyond Tailwind: cva (~2KB) and tailwind-merge (~3KB) — total of 5 deps (tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer, cva, tailwind-merge)
NEGATIVE: CVA introduces a new mental model on top of Tailwind — developers must learn both the utility-first approach and the variant definition API
NEGATIVE: tailwind.config.js token configuration is still required and still subject to drift from design specs — same audit discipline needed as alt-tailwind

NEGATIVE: Slightly more upfront scaffolding: each shared component (Button, Input, Card, Badge, Chip) needs a CVA definition file before it can be used, adding ~1–2 hours of initial setup per component

structured.rationale: Four alternatives were selected to cover the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full CSS-in-JS, with two Tailwind variants included because the utility-first approach is the dominant pattern for greenfield React projects in 2024 and the CVA extension meaningfully changes the tradeoff profile. styled-components was included as the canonical CSS-in-JS representative despite its runtime cost, because its ThemeProvider pattern is the strongest token-enforcement mechanism. CSS Modules was included as the mandatory "minimal change" alternative — it requires no new dependencies and is natively supported by all bundlers. Excluded alternatives: (1) Sass/SCSS — adds a preprocessor dependency with no meaningful advantage over CSS custom properties for this project's token needs; (2) Emotion — functionally similar to styled-components with the same RSC incompatibility and runtime cost, redundant to include both; (3) Vanilla Extract — zero-runtime CSS-in-JS is compelling but adds significant build complexity (Vite plugin required) and has a smaller ecosystem, making it a poor fit for a greenfield project prioritizing delivery speed; (4) UnoCSS — too experimental for a financial application requiring long-term maintainability. The recommendation favors alt-tailwind-cva because it uniquely satisfies the WCAG enforcement, performance, and design token consistency constraints simultaneously, at the cost of only 2 additional small dependencies.
structured.recommendation: alt-tailwind-cva — Tailwind CSS + CVA is the recommended approach for CashPulse. It is the only alternative that simultaneously satisfies all three critical constraints: (1) **Performance** — zero runtime CSS generation means all micro-interactions, screen transitions, and chart renders operate purely on pre-extracted CSS with no JS overhead; (2) **WCAG 2.1 AA** — CVA bakes focus rings, minimum touch targets, and ARIA-compatible state classes into each component's variant definition, making compliance structural rather than conventional; (3) **Design Consistency** — the tailwind.config.js theme extension is the single source of truth for all 9 design tokens (Inter, 4px unit, 5 radius values, 2 shadow values, blue-600), and CVA's typed variant API prevents off-token usage at compile time. The 5-dependency footprint is the lowest of any approach that provides this level of structural enforcement. If the team has strong CSS Modules familiarity and wants to minimize onboarding time, alt-css-modules is a sound fallback — it achieves the performance goals equally well and adds zero dependencies, at the cost of manual breakpoint repetition and convention-only token enforcement.
